1:
"Forasmuch as many have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us." The witnesses note the polished preface — unique among the Gospels — and the careful method it announces. The grammatical-historical voices admire its literary finish. Ellicott: "the finished structure of the sentences as compared with the simpler openings of the other Gospels." Robertson: "This Preface by Luke is in splendid literary Koine and is not surpassed by those in any Greek writer." On "the many," the witnesses differ: Barnes thinks them "fragments of history, or narratives of detached sayings," not the canonical Gospels; Wesley: "He does not mean St. Matthew or Mark"; Ellicott: "The many may have included St. Matthew and St. Mark, but we cannot say... There is no tone of disparagement." Robertson notes Luke "does not mean to cast reflection on those who preceded him."

The fathers read the preface as establishing the Gospel's certainty. Eusebius: Luke wrote "because many others had rashly taken upon themselves to give accounts of those things of which he had a more certain knowledge." Ambrose contrasts the inspired with the presumptuous: "many attempted to write Gospels which the good moneychanger refuses to pass" (Basilides, Matthias), for "they who have attempted to set forth these things... have not succeeded." Bede: "men who were not endued with the gift of the Holy Spirit... have rather set forth in order a relation of events, than woven a true history."

The puritan Henry draws the dedication's lesson: Luke writes "not as to his patron... but as to his pupil, to learn it." He notes Luke "was moved by the Holy Ghost, not only to the writing, but in the writing of it; but in both he was moved as a reasonable creature, and not as a mere machine." The things written were "most surely believed among all Christians... things which have been long most surely believed."

The Reformed Calvin guards the certainty: "by laying claim to certainty as to the facts, he modestly denies their title to full and unshaken confidence" — not accusing the earlier writers of imposture, but counteracting "historical sketches which... would afterwards, if they had not been promptly counteracted, have done serious injury to the faith." The witnesses agree: Luke, a careful historian moved by the Spirit, opens his Gospel by setting his accurate, ordered narrative on the firm foundation of what was surely believed, that Theophilus and all believers might rest on its certainty.
2:
"Even as they delivered them unto us, which from the beginning were eyewitnesses, and ministers of the word." The witnesses note Luke's reliance on the apostolic eyewitnesses. Gill identifies them as "the twelve apostles, and seventy disciples; who handed down to others the accounts of the birth, life, and death of Christ." Robertson stresses the reliability of the tradition: "Tradition has come to have a meaning of unreliability with us, but that is not the idea here. Luke means to say that the handing down was dependable, not mere wives' fables... Here we have both written and oral sources. Luke had access to both kinds."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the candour: "There is something noticeable in the candour with which the writer disclaims the character of an eyewitness. The word delivered is the same as that used by St. Paul when he speaks of the history of the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 11:23) and of the Resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:3)." On "ministers of the word," he explains the term describes "the work of an attendant... in its later sense, teaching preparatory to baptism," the "word" meaning "the gospel," not yet the personal Word of John. Robertson notes "eyewitnesses" (autoptai) "is an old Greek word... it means seeing with one's own eyes," and "from the beginning apparently refers to the beginning of the ministry of Jesus as was true of the apostles (Acts 1:22)."

The eastern fathers read "the word" more richly. Cyril: in saying the apostles were "eyewitnesses of the word," Luke "agrees with John, who says, The Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we saw His glory. For the Word by means of the flesh was made visible." Origen drew the distinction between the voice heard and the Word seen: "in the Gospel, it is not the voice that is perceived, but the Word, which is more excellent than the voice." The Greek Exposition notes the eyewitnesses "had a commission to transmit the truth."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Luke grounds his narrative on the dependable tradition handed down by those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the word — the apostles and first disciples. The candour with which Luke disclaims being himself an eyewitness, and his reliance on those who were, strengthen rather than weaken his authority, for he builds on the testimony of those who saw and heard. The "delivered" and "ministers of the word" are the technical terms of apostolic transmission. The verse displays the historical foundation of the Gospel: not invention, but the faithful conveyance of what the eyewitnesses beheld of the Word made flesh.
3:
"It seemed good to me also, having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first, to write unto thee in order, most excellent Theophilus." The witnesses note Luke's careful method, his inspiration, and the identity of Theophilus. On the key phrase, Ellicott: "Better, having traced (or investigated) all things from their source. The verb used is one which implies following the course of events step by step." Calvin: the Greek verb "is taken metaphorically from those who tread in the footsteps of others, that nothing may escape them... Luke intended to express his close and laborious investigation." Robertson: "Luke got himself ready to write before he began by full and accurate knowledge of the subject."

The witnesses stress that Luke's inspiration did not set aside his natural faculties. Barnes: "in religion God does not set aside our natural faculties. He calls us to look at evidence; to examine accounts; to make up our own minds... God, by his Holy Spirit, presided over his faculties, directed them, and kept him from error." Clarke: "this gift was never designed to set aside the use of those faculties with which he has already endued the soul... he set himself, by impartial inquiry and diligent investigation, to find the whole truth... and the Spirit of God presided over and directed his inquiries, so that he discovered the whole truth, and was preserved from every particle of error."

On "in order," the witnesses note it means orderly and full, not strictly chronological. Barnes: "it means distinctly, particularly, in opposition to the confused and broken accounts." Ellicott: "it does not necessarily follow, where the order in St. Luke varies from that of the other Gospels, that it is therefore the true order." On Theophilus, the witnesses agree he was a real person of rank: Ellicott, "the adjective is the same as that used of Felix... and implies at least high social position, if not official rank"; Robertson, "Ramsay holds that most excellent was a title... perhaps a Knight." Theophilus, "Friend of God," "might well be taken by a Christian convert at his baptism."

The witnesses agree: Luke, having diligently traced all things from their source, wrote an orderly and full narrative for Theophilus — a real person of rank, probably a Gentile convert. His careful investigation, far from contradicting his inspiration, was the very means the Spirit used: God presided over his diligent inquiry and kept him from error. The verse displays the harmony of human diligence and divine inspiration in the writing of Scripture, and the firm historical basis on which the Gospel rests.
4:
"That thou mightest know the certainty of those things, wherein thou hast been instructed." The witnesses note the aim of Luke's Gospel — assurance for the already-instructed. Gill: "that he might be more strongly assured of and more firmly established in the truths of the Gospel." Robertson: "Full knowledge... The certainty (ten asphaleian). Make no slip... Luke promises a reliable narrative. Theophilus shall know that the faith which he has embraced has an impregnable historical foundation (Plummer)."

On "instructed," the witnesses note the word of catechetical teaching. Ellicott: "The verb used is that from which are formed the words catechise, catechumen... and implies oral teaching — in its later sense, teaching preparatory to baptism. The passage is important as showing that such instruction mainly turned on the facts of our Lord's life, death, and resurrection." Robertson: "Katecheo is to sound down, to din, to instruct, to give oral instruction... Whether Theophilus was still a catechumen is not known." Clarke: Theophilus "had already received the first elements of the Christian doctrine, but had not as yet been completely grounded in them."

The aim of certainty the witnesses draw out for all believers. Clarke: "Those who content themselves with that knowledge of the doctrines of Christ which they receive from catechisms and schoolmasters... are never likely to arrive at such a knowledge of the truth as will make them wise unto salvation... Every man should labor to acquire the most correct knowledge, and indubitable certainty, of those doctrines on which he stakes his eternal salvation." Henry (under v.1) had drawn the same: "those who have been well instructed in the things of God when they were young should afterwards give diligence to know the certainty of those things, to know not only what we believe, but why we believe it."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the preface. The witnesses agree: Luke wrote that Theophilus — already orally instructed in the facts of the gospel — might know their certainty, resting his faith on an impregnable historical foundation. The aim of the written Gospel is assurance: to confirm and establish what catechetical teaching had begun. The verse teaches that the believer should not be content with elementary or second-hand knowledge but should press on to a settled, well-grounded certainty of the truths on which his salvation depends. The Gospel is given not merely to inform but to assure — that we may know not only what we believe but why, and so be fortified against every doubt. The certainty of the gospel facts is the rock on which faith securely stands.
5:
"There was in the days of Herod, the king of Judaea, a certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia: and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elisabeth." The witnesses note the time, the priestly parents, and the prophetic significance of Herod's reign. The fathers read the date as a sign of Messiah's coming. Bede: "the time of Herod, i.e. of a foreign king, bears witness to our Lord's coming, for it had been foretold, The scepter shall not depart from Judah... until Shiloh come"; the government having passed "by the command of Augustus Caesar to this same Herod, a foreigner, in whose thirty-first year... Shiloh came." Chrysostom notes Luke begins with John, "relating one marvelous event before another, the less before the greater. For since a virgin was about to become a mother, it had been fore-ordained by grace that the old should previously conceive."

Ambrose commends the praise of John's parents and ancestry: "not only the characters of the men themselves, but of their parents also, ought to be praised... a descent not exalted by secular power, but venerable from its sanctity." Bede: "John was allotted a Priestly tribe, that he might with the more authority herald forth a change of priesthood." Of the course of Abia (the eighth of David's twenty-four courses), Bede notes "the first preacher of the new covenant was born with the rights of the eighth lot," the eighth signifying "the sacrament of our Lord's or our resurrection."

The puritan Henry draws out the providence: the reign of "Herod the king, who was a foreigner... is taken notice of to show that the sceptre was quite departed from Judah, and therefore that now was the time for Shiloh to come." He marks the honour to two houses: "No families in the world were ever so honoured of God as those of Aaron and David... the gospel again puts honour upon both in their latter days, on that of Aaron in John Baptist, on that of David in Christ." Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, explains the courses of priests and corrects the error that Zacharias was high priest: he "is said to have been but of the eighth course."

The witnesses agree: the narrative opens in the days of Herod the foreign king — a sign, by Jacob's prophecy, that the sceptre had departed from Judah and Shiloh was at hand — with a righteous priestly couple, Zacharias of the course of Abia and Elizabeth of Aaron's daughters. The honour put on the houses of Aaron (in John) and David (in Christ) in their latter days, and the providential timing, set the stage for the births that would inaugurate the gospel age.
6:
"And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless." The witnesses dwell on the genuine piety of John's parents. Ambrose marks the force of "before God": "a man by affecting a popular good-will might seem just to me, but not be just before God, if that justice instead of springing from simpleness of heart, was a mere presence carried on by flattery. Perfect then is the praise, that a man is just before God; for he only is perfect who is approved by Him who cannot be deceived." He distinguishes "the commandment" and "the justification": "when we obey the command of heaven we walk in the commandments of the Lord, when we observe justice we seem to possess the justification of the Lord."

The puritan Henry draws out the marks of their righteousness: "they were both righteous before God; they were so in his sight whose judgment, we are sure, is according to truth." He notes it was evidenced "by the course and tenour of their conversations; they showed it, not by their talk, but by their works"; that "their devotions and their conversations agreed"; that "they were universal in their obedience"; and that, "though they were not sinless, yet they were blameless; nobody could charge them with any open scandalous sin." He adds the special fitness for ministers: "it is especially requisite that the priests, the Lord's ministers, should with their yoke-fellows be righteous before God, that they may be examples to the flock."

The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical voices guard the sense of "blameless" against perfectionism. The righteousness is sincere and real, not sinless; "blameless" means free from open scandal, not flawless. Their walking "in all the commandments" was their constant aim and endeavour, not a claim of having never fallen short.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Zacharias and Elizabeth were truly righteous — not sinless, but sincerely and really godly, approved by the God who cannot be deceived. Their righteousness was real ("before God"), evidenced in their walk, consistent between devotion and conduct, universal in its aim, and blameless in the sense of free from open scandal. The verse both honours the genuine piety of John's parents — a fit preparation for the forerunner's birth — and teaches the marks of true righteousness: not mere reputation among men, but heart-sincerity before God, shown in a consistent and universal (though not flawless) obedience. Ministers especially, the witnesses note, must with their households be righteous before God, that they may be examples to the flock. The righteous couple, long childless, are the godly soil in which God's gracious answer (v.13) would spring.
7:
"And they had no child, because that Elisabeth was barren, and they both were now well stricken in years." The witnesses note the long childlessness and its providential design. The fathers (under v.5) read the barrenness as ordained for instruction. Chrysostom: "Not that their barrenness was the effect of sin, since all were just and virtuous, but ordained rather for our benefit, that when you saw a virgin giving birth to the Lord, you might not be faithless... with respect to the womb of the barren." Theophylact: "that you might learn that the law of God seeks not a bodily increase of sons but a spiritual."

The puritan Henry draws out the trial and its honour: "Children are a heritage of the Lord. But there are many of his heirs in a married state, that yet are denied this heritage... many there are, who are righteous before God, and, if they had children, would bring them up in his fear, who yet are not thus blessed." He notes the pattern of eminent men born to long-barren mothers: "Many eminent persons were born of mothers that had been long childless, as Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Samson, Samuel, and so here John Baptist, to make their birth the more remarkable and the blessing of it the more valuable to their parents."

The barrenness and old age the witnesses (with Calvin, under v.13) note as setting up the trial of Zacharias's faith and the magnitude of the coming miracle. Like Abraham and Sarah, the couple had passed the natural time of childbearing; their case seemed hopeless. The very hopelessness magnifies the grace of God, who keeps his people "long waiting for mercy" and "sometimes is pleased to recompense them for their patience by doubling the worth of it when it comes" (Henry).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the long childlessness of Zacharias and Elizabeth — Elizabeth barren, both now aged — was no mark of sin but a providential preparation, both for the trial of faith and for the magnifying of the miracle to come. As with the barren wives of the patriarchs (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel) and the mothers of Samson and Samuel, the hopeless case set the stage for an extraordinary birth, that the forerunner's coming might be the more remarkable and the parents' blessing the more valued. The barrenness also, the fathers note, prepares faith to believe a greater wonder — the virgin's conception. The verse marks the human impossibility that God's grace would overturn, teaching that God often keeps his people waiting, and rewards their patience by doubling the worth of the blessing when at last it comes.
8:
"And it came to pass, that while he executed the priest's office before God in the order of his course." The witnesses note the occasion of the angelic visitation — Zacharias serving in his appointed turn. Bede (the lone father here) reads the setting devotionally, of the soul drawing near to God in its ministry. Robertson and the grammatical voices explain the order of the courses: each of the twenty-four divisions of priests served in the temple twice a year for a week, and Zacharias's course of Abia (v.5) had now come round.

The phrase "before God" the witnesses note as marking the solemnity of the priestly service. Zacharias ministered in the temple, in the presence of God, in the appointed order of his course — the regular, ordained worship of the old covenant. It was in the midst of this faithful, routine service that the extraordinary visitation came. Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning (under v.5 and v.9), details the casting of lots by which the various priestly duties were assigned within each course.

The occasion the witnesses (with Henry, under v.5) read as a lesson that God meets his servants in the path of duty. Zacharias was not seeking a vision but faithfully performing his appointed ministry when the angel appeared. The regular service of God, done in order and in his presence, was the setting for the announcement that would open the gospel age.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: it was while Zacharias faithfully executed the priest's office in his appointed course, in the presence of God, that the angelic visitation came. The verse marks the solemn, ordained setting of the old-covenant worship — the regular service into which the extraordinary now broke. God met his servant in the path of duty, in the midst of his appointed ministry; the answer to long prayer (v.13) came not while Zacharias sought a sign but while he served. The detail of "the order of his course" anchors the narrative in the historical realities of temple worship and prepares for the moment of incense (v.9) when, alone in the holy place, the priest would receive the message of the forerunner's birth. The faithful discharge of ordinary duty was the occasion of an extraordinary grace. (The Cyril of Jerusalem fragment here is a broader catechetical aside.)
9:
"According to the custom of the priest's office, his lot was to burn incense when he went into the temple of the Lord." The witnesses note the sacred duty assigned by lot and its spiritual meaning. Gill and Lightfoot explain the custom: among the many priestly duties, the burning of incense was assigned by lot, and it was esteemed a high honour, often falling to a priest but once in a lifetime. The incense was offered on the golden altar in the holy place, while the people prayed without (v.10).

The Reformed Calvin reflects on the propriety of the priest's prayer at this moment, anticipating v.13: as Zacharias entered "in the name of all the people," he prayed; and Calvin takes his prayer (later said to be heard) as the long-continued petition of his life rather than a sudden private request for a son. The burning of incense, in Scripture and in the witnesses' reading, is the symbol of prayer ascending to God (Psalm 141:2; Revelation 8:3-4): "Let my prayer be set forth before thee as incense." Zacharias's offering of incense thus fittingly accompanied the prayers of the people, and was the setting for the answer to prayer that the angel announced.

The lot the witnesses note as the providence of God. That this particular duty, on this particular day, fell to Zacharias by lot was no accident but the ordering of God, who appointed the moment of the great announcement. The high and rare honour of offering incense in the holy place became the occasion of an honour higher still — the visitation of an angel and the message of the forerunner's birth.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Zacharias's lot, according to the priestly custom, was to burn incense in the holy place — a high and rare honour, esteemed the privilege of a lifetime. The incense, the appointed symbol of prayer ascending to God, fittingly accompanied the people's prayers (v.10) and set the scene for the answer to prayer the angel would bring. The casting of the lot, far from being chance, was the providence of God ordering the moment of the great announcement. The verse anchors the narrative in the realities of temple worship and prepares for the angelic appearance at the altar of incense (v.11). The priest who entered to offer the symbol of prayer would receive, in that very place, the assurance that his prayer was heard — the incense of intercession answered by the message of grace.
10:
"And the whole multitude of the people were praying without at the time of incense." The witnesses note the praying congregation and the symbolism of the incense. Gill and the grammatical voices explain the scene: while the priest offered incense within the holy place, the assembled worshippers prayed in the courts without, their prayers ascending together with the fragrant smoke. Lightfoot details the hours of the temple service.

The joining of the people's prayer with the priest's incense the witnesses read as the appointed symbol of intercession. The incense ascending from the golden altar figured the prayers of the saints rising to God (Revelation 8:3-4); and the multitude praying without, at the very hour of incense, embodied the worship of the whole people gathered to the house of God. The scene displays the old-covenant pattern of mediated worship: the priest within, the people without, the incense and the prayers ascending together.

The setting also marks the public, solemn occasion of the great announcement. It was not in private but at the hour of the temple's evening or morning service, with the whole multitude at prayer, that God chose to send his angel — though the vision itself was seen by Zacharias alone within the holy place. The praying people without, awaiting the priest's return and blessing, would soon marvel at his delay (v.21) and perceive that he had seen a vision.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: while Zacharias offered incense within, the whole multitude of the people prayed without, at the hour of incense — the prayers of the congregation ascending together with the fragrant smoke, in the appointed symbol of intercession. The scene embodies the old-covenant pattern of mediated worship, the priest within and the people without, and sets the public, solemn occasion of the angelic announcement. The incense rising from the altar figured the prayers of the saints (Revelation 8:3-4); and at this very hour, when the people's prayers ascended, God sent his answer in the message of the forerunner's birth. The praying multitude, awaiting the priest's blessing, would soon witness his speechless return and know that something extraordinary had befallen him in the holy place (vv.21-22). The hour of prayer became the hour of grace.
11:
"And there appeared unto him an angel of the Lord standing on the right side of the altar of incense." The witnesses note the angelic appearance and its place. The fathers read the visitation and its setting richly. Chrysostom: the angel appears at the altar of incense, where the priest ministered. Origen, Ambrose, and Bede reflect on the right side as the side of favour and good tidings. Augustine and Damascene meditate on the nature of angelic ministry. Ambrose notes the fitness of the announcement coming in the temple, at the altar, in the hour of prayer.

The grammatical voices supply the historical setting. The "altar of incense" was the golden altar in the holy place, before the veil; the angel stood "on the right side," which Gill and others note was the south side, between the altar and the candlestick — the place of honour and grace. The appearance came suddenly to Zacharias as he ministered alone within.

The angel's standing at the altar of incense the witnesses connect to the symbolism of prayer. As the incense figured the prayers of the saints ascending (v.10), so the angel — the bearer of God's answer — stood at the altar of intercession, at the very moment the priest offered the symbol of prayer. The place and time of the visitation thus declared that prayer was heard: the angel of the Lord came to the altar of incense to announce the answer (v.13).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: an angel of the Lord appeared to Zacharias at the altar of incense, standing on the right side — the place of honour and favour. The visitation, coming suddenly as the priest ministered alone within the holy place, at the very hour of prayer, declared by its place and time that prayer was heard. The angel stood at the altar of intercession, the bearer of God's gracious answer, where the incense of prayer ascended. The fathers read the right side as the side of good tidings, fitting the joyful message to come. The verse marks the breaking-in of the heavenly upon the earthly worship: into the solemn, ordained service of the temple, at the altar where prayer ascended, came the messenger of God to announce the forerunner's birth and the dawn of the gospel age. The answer to long prayer stood at the altar.
12:
"And when Zacharias saw him, he was troubled, and fear fell upon him." The witnesses note the priest's terror at the heavenly apparition. Gill and the grammatical voices observe that angelic appearances, especially in the holy place, were unusual and awe-inspiring, and that Zacharias's fear was the natural reaction of mortal flesh confronted with the heavenly.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of holy fear: "the glory of God, it ought to be observed, is not so appalling to the saints as to swallow them up entirely with dread, but only to cast them down from a foolish confidence, that they may behold him with humility. As soon, therefore, as God has abased the pride of the flesh in those who believe in him, he stretches out his hand to raise them up." He contrasts the saints with the reprobate: the saints' fear gives way to comfort, "whereas they are greatly mistaken who, in order to enjoy peace, hide themselves from the face of God."

Wesley notes the difference in spirit between Zacharias's later doubt and Mary's faith (which he develops at v.18 and v.34), but here the fear is simply the common effect of a divine visitation. The terror that fell on Zacharias was not yet unbelief but the awe proper to a man suddenly aware of the presence of a heavenly messenger.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Zacharias was troubled and afraid at the sudden appearance of the angel — the natural reaction of mortal flesh to the heavenly, especially in the solemn place of the holy of holies. The fear, Calvin notes, is the proper effect of God's glory upon the saints: not to swallow them in despair (as it does the reprobate), but to cast down the pride of the flesh and humble them, that they may behold God with reverence — after which he stretches out his hand to raise them up (which the angel does in v.13, "Fear not"). The verse marks the awe with which Zacharias received the visitation, an awe the angel would at once turn to comfort. Holy fear, the witnesses teach, is the beginning of a right approach to God; and the God who humbles his servants by his glory also reassures them by his grace. The trembling priest would soon hear the word of peace.
13:
"But the angel said unto him, Fear not, Zacharias: for thy prayer is heard; and thy wife Elisabeth shall bear thee a son, and thou shalt call his name John." The witnesses note the gracious reassurance, the answered prayer, and the meaning of the name. On "thy prayer is heard," the witnesses differ. Ellicott takes it of the kingdom: "the words imply a prayer on the part of Zacharias, not that he might have a son (that hope appears to have died out long before), but that the Kingdom of God might come. Praying for this he receives more than he asks." Clarke takes it of both: "thou shalt have a son, and Israel shall be saved." Calvin takes it as "his prayer was at length heard, which he had poured out before God for a long period." Robertson notes the timeless aorist: "was heard when made, and so is heard now."

On the name John, the witnesses unfold its meaning. Ellicott: "the meaning of the Hebrew word is Jehovah is gracious," so "the announcement of the name was in itself a pledge of the outpouring of the grace of God." Robertson: "The mention of the name should have helped Zacharias to believe." Calvin sees the name as commending "not the grace which God bestowed upon him as a private individual, but that grace which his mission would bring to all"; and notes its weight is increased by its being given before birth.

The angel's "Fear not" the witnesses read as the word of grace that turns terror to comfort. Wesley: "the prayers of pious worshippers come up with acceptance before God... An answer of peace was here returned, when the case seemed to be most helpless. Let us wait patiently for the Lord, and leave to his own wisdom the time and manner wherein he will appear for us." Calvin (under v.12) had noted that God, having humbled Zacharias by his glory, now "stretches out his hand to raise him up."

The witnesses agree: the angel stills Zacharias's fear and announces that his prayer is heard — whether his long petition for a son, or his prayer (as priest) for the redemption of Israel, or both — and that he shall have a son named John, "Jehovah is gracious," a name that is itself a pledge of the grace his mission would bring to all. The verse displays the mercy of God, who answers prayer in his own time when the case seems most hopeless, and turns the trembling of his servant into joy by the word "Fear not." The named son, given before his birth, would be the herald of the grace of God to the whole people.
14:
"And thou shalt have joy and gladness; and many shall rejoice at his birth." The witnesses note the joy that John's birth would bring, both to his parents and to many. Gill: the joy would be "not only because of his having a son; but because this his son would be the prophet of the Highest." Robertson notes "gladness" (agalliasis) is "a word for extreme exultation." Ellicott connects it to the priest's prayer: "He had been seeking the joy of many rather than his own, and now the one was to be fruitful in the other."

The Reformed Calvin draws out that the joy would be public, not merely domestic: "the joy would not be domestic, enjoyed by the parents alone, or confined within private walls, but shared alike by strangers, to whom the advantage of his birth should be made known. It is as if the angel had said that a son would be born not to Zacharias alone, but would be the Teacher and Prophet of the whole people." He notes the people "rejoiced that a prophet was born to them, by whose ministry they were led to the hope of salvation." Calvin sharply rebukes the abuse of this text to justify the carnal festivities of the feast of John's nativity.

Clarke draws the lesson on godly children: "A child of prayer and faith is likely to be a source of comfort to his parents. Were proper attention paid to this point, there would be fewer disobedient children in the world." And on the many: John "shall be the minister of God for good to multitudes, who shall, through his preaching, be turned from the error of their ways."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: John's birth would bring joy and exultation not only to his aged parents but to many — for he would be the prophet of the Highest, by whose ministry the people would be led to the hope of salvation. The joy, Calvin stresses, would be public and far-reaching, not confined to the home, since John was given not merely as a son to Zacharias but as a teacher and prophet to the whole nation. The verse both promises the parents the deep joy of a child of prayer (a fruit of their long petition) and announces the wider joy his prophetic ministry would bring. The rejoicing at John's birth anticipates the greater joy of the Messiah whom he would announce; the herald's coming was itself glad tidings. The child of prayer would be a source of comfort to many, turning multitudes to their God.
15:
"For he shall be great in the sight of the Lord, and shall drink neither wine nor strong drink; and he shall be filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb." The witnesses note John's greatness, his Nazarite abstinence, and his early fullness of the Spirit. The fathers read his greatness as of soul. Ambrose: "the greatness signified is not of the body, but of the soul. Greatness in the sight of the Lord is greatness of soul, greatness of virtue." Bede explains the Nazarite vow: "it was part of the law of the Nazarites to give up wine and strong drink... rightly then is he, from whom all drunkenness with wine is utterly put away, filled with the grace of the Spirit." Ambrose marks the contrast: "On whomsoever the Holy Spirit is poured, in him there is fullness of great virtue; as in St. John, who before he was born... bore witness to the grace of the Spirit."

The grammatical voices supply the detail. Ellicott: "The child now promised was to grow up as a Nazarite (Numbers 6:4)... He was to be what Samson had been, and probably Samuel also." He draws the contrast Paul makes: "the absence of the lower form of stimulation implied the capacity for the higher enthusiasm which was the gift of God. The same contrast is seen in St. Paul's words, Be not drunk with wine, but be filled with the Spirit (Ephesians 5:18)." Robertson notes "even from his mother's womb" is "a manifest Hebraism."

The Reformed Calvin guards against misreading the abstinence as the substance of holiness: "we must not on this ground imagine that the worship of God consists in abstinence from wine... God was pleased to distinguish his servant by this visible token." On "filled with the Holy Ghost," he explains it means "the pre-eminent and very uncommon abundance of the gifts of the Spirit," John bearing "from the womb a token of future rank." Origen: "even now the spirit and power of John must come upon the soul, before it believes in Jesus Christ."

The witnesses agree: John would be great — not in body or worldly rank but in soul, in the sight of the Lord — a lifelong Nazarite abstaining from wine, and filled with the Holy Ghost even from the womb. His abstinence was the visible token of his consecration, not the substance of his holiness; and his early fullness of the Spirit (uncommon abundance of spiritual gifts) marked him from birth for his great prophetic office. The verse displays the forerunner's character: separated, self-denying, Spirit-filled — fit to prepare the way of the Lord, and a pattern of the higher enthusiasm of the Spirit that needs no lower stimulation.
16:
"And many of the children of Israel shall he turn to the Lord their God." The witnesses note John's converting ministry and its divine source. Gill: "great multitudes of them flocked unto him, attended on his ministry, believed his doctrine, and submitted to his baptism, but not all... however, some there were, and many too, that were converted under his ministry." Barnes: "By repentance. He shall call them from their sins, and persuade them to forsake them." Ellicott connects it to Malachi: "The opening words of the message of the New Covenant spring out of the closing words of the last of the prophets (Malachi 4:6), and point to the revival of the Elijah ministry."

The Reformed Calvin draws a rich doctrine of the ministry from this verse. He notes the dissolute state of the church that needed such conversion: "those in whom conversion to God could take place must have been apostates." Then he guards the honour of God in the work: though conversion "is not only God's own work, but surpasses even the creation of men," yet "when the Lord bestows so great praise on the outward doctrine, he does not separate it from the secret influence of his Spirit." Ministers are God's instrument: "those whom God, by the aid of the minister, converts to himself, are said to be converted by the minister, because he is nothing more than the hand of God." And the efficacy is selective: John "did not indiscriminately bring all back... but only brought those back whom it pleased the Lord effectually to call" — for "faith cometh by hearing" (Romans 10:17), yet "those only to whom the Lord inwardly reveals his arm" believe.

Wesley draws the encouragement to preachers: "None therefore need be ashamed of preaching like John the Baptist." Clarke cross-references the fulfilment in John's ministry (Luke 3:10ff).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: John would turn many of Israel to the Lord their God — a converting ministry fulfilling Malachi's promise of the Elijah to come (Malachi 4:6). Calvin draws the great lesson on the ministry: conversion is God's own work, surpassing creation, yet wrought through the minister as God's instrument and hand; the outward doctrine is efficacious not by itself but joined to the secret influence of the Spirit, and turns only those whom God effectually calls. The verse both foretells John's success in calling Israel to repentance and teaches the right understanding of the ministry — the preacher labouring, God converting; the word the instrument, the Spirit the power. "The Lord their God" to whom John turns them is, the witnesses note (anticipating v.17), Christ himself, whose forerunner he was.
17:
"And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord." The witnesses note John as forerunner and his Elijah-like ministry. On "before him," Barnes and Robertson note the antecedent: "him refers to the Lord their God in the previous verse. If so, then it will follow that the Messiah was the Lord God of Israel" (Barnes). Calvin draws the same: "the angel declares John to be the usher or standard-bearer of the eternal God, we learn from it the eternal divinity of Christ."

On "the spirit and power of Elias," the witnesses reject any transmigration. Calvin: "By the words I understand the power or excellency of the Spirit, with which Elijah was endued; for we must not here indulge in a dream like that of Pythagoras, that the soul of the prophet passed into the body of John, but the same Spirit of God, who had acted efficaciously in Elijah, afterwards exerted a similar power... in the Baptist." Lightfoot: "The Baptist is Elias, as our Saviour was David; that is, the antitype." Robertson: "John will deny that he is actually Elijah in person, as they expected (John 1:21), but Jesus will call him Elijah in spirit (Mark 9:12)."

On turning "the hearts of the fathers to the children," the witnesses read it of reconciliation and conversion. Barnes: John "came that he might allay these animosities and produce better feeling. By directing them all to one Master, the Messiah... The effect of true religion on a family will always be to produce harmony." Calvin reads it of restoring the people "from discord to reconciliation," the agreement being grounded in "the wisdom of the just" — that is, faith — "accursed then be the peace and unity by which men agree among themselves apart from God."

The witnesses agree: John goes before the Lord (Christ, whose forerunner he is — a proof of Christ's deity) in the spirit and power of Elijah — not by transmigration but by the same Spirit's power — to reconcile the divided, turn the disobedient to the wisdom of faith, and make ready a people prepared for the Lord. The verse displays John's office as forerunner: to restore, by his preaching of repentance, a people fitted to receive the Messiah, and to revive the Elijah ministry foretold by Malachi. The reconciliation he works is grounded in faith, the wisdom of the just; and the people he prepares are made ready, by repentance and faith, for the coming of the Lord.
18:
"And Zacharias said unto the angel, Whereby shall I know this? for I am an old man, and my wife well stricken in years." The witnesses note Zacharias's doubt and contrast it with Mary's faith. Chrysostom: "Considering his own age, and moreover the barrenness of his wife, Zacharias doubted... whenever God declares any thing, it becomes us to receive it in faith, and moreover, disputes of this kind are the mark of a rebellious spirit." Robertson: "It was too good to be true and Zacharias demanded proof... He had prayed for this blessing and was now sceptical." Wesley draws the contrast: "In how different a spirit did the blessed virgin say, How shall this be? Zacharias disbelieved the fact: Mary had no doubt of the thing; but only inquired concerning the manner of it."

The Reformed Calvin addresses the difficulty of why Zacharias was punished when Abraham, Gideon, and Mary asked similar questions: "if the words only are considered, either all were equally to blame, or Zacharias did nothing wrong. But as the actions and words of men must be judged from the state of the heart, we ought rather to abide by the judgment of God, to whom the hidden secrets of the heart are naked." He diagnoses the root: "The reason why Zacharias doubted was, that, stopping at the ordinary course of nature, he ascribed less than he ought to have done to the power of God... he went so far as to reason with himself, whether God, who had undoubtedly spoken to him, should be regarded as worthy of credit." Yet Calvin notes the limit of his unbelief: "while he held the root and foundation of faith, he hesitated only on one point."

The grammatical voices note the demand for a sign. Barnes: "The testimony of an angel, and in such a place, should have been proof enough; but men are slow to believe... As a consequence of not believing, he was struck dumb." Clarke: "it is impious, when we are convinced that it is his word, to demand a sign or pledge for its fulfillment."

The witnesses agree: Zacharias, looking only at the ordinary course of nature and his own age, doubted the angel's word and demanded a sign — a doubt rooted in ascribing too little to the power of God. His unbelief, Calvin notes, was partial (he held the root of faith but wavered on this point), yet culpable, for he questioned whether God, having spoken, deserved credit. The contrast with Mary's question (v.34) is instructive: she believed the fact and asked only the manner; he disbelieved the fact and demanded proof. The verse displays the weakness of faith that limits God to nature's bounds, and prepares for the sign of dumbness (v.20) by which God would both chastise and confirm.
19:
"And the angel answering said unto him, I am Gabriel, that stand in the presence of God; and am sent to speak unto thee, and to shew thee these glad tidings." The witnesses note the angel's self-identification and the authority it gives his message. Gill and the grammatical voices observe that Gabriel ("the strength of God") was the same angel who had announced the seventy weeks to Daniel (Daniel 8:16; 9:21), and that his standing in the presence of God marks his high rank and the certainty of his message.

The angel's rebuke of Zacharias's doubt the witnesses read in the self-identification. Bede (under v.18 area): "If it were man who promised these miracles, one might with impunity demand a sign, but when an angel promises, it is then not right to doubt." Chrysostom: "When you hear that I am sent from God, you should deem none of the things which are said to you to be of man, for I speak not of myself, but declare the message of Him who sends me. And this is the merit and excellence of a messenger to relate nothing of his own." Gregory reflects on the angels' dual presence: "when angels come to us, they so outwardly fulfill their ministry, as at the same time inwardly to be never absent from His sight."

The "glad tidings" the witnesses note as the gracious character of the message. Despite Zacharias's doubt, the angel reaffirms that he brings good news — the birth of the forerunner. Calvin (under v.13) had noted the angel "soothes Zacharias" with the assurance of God's favour. The self-naming of Gabriel both rebukes the doubt (for who could doubt a message from one who stands in God's presence?) and confirms the certainty of the word.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel answers Zacharias's doubt by naming himself Gabriel, who stands in the presence of God and is sent to bring glad tidings — a self-identification that both rebukes the doubt and confirms the certainty of the message. To doubt a word from one who stands before God, and who relates nothing of his own but only the message of him who sent him, is unreasonable; the high rank and divine commission of the messenger guarantee the truth of his tidings. Gabriel, the announcer of Daniel's seventy weeks, now announces the forerunner of the Messiah — a continuity that links the prophecy and its fulfilment. The verse displays the authority of the heavenly messenger and the graciousness of his news, and prepares for the sign of dumbness (v.20) by which the doubting priest would be chastised and the word confirmed.
20:
"And, behold, thou shalt be dumb, and not able to speak, until the day that these things shall be performed, because thou believest not my words, which shall be fulfilled in their season." The witnesses note the sign of dumbness as both punishment and confirmation. Chrysostom: "That the bonds might be transferred from the powers of generation to the vocal organs. From no regard to the priesthood was he spared, but for this reason was the more smitten, because in a matter of faith he ought to have set an example to others." Theophylact notes the fitness: "as disobedient, he incurs the penalty of deafness; as an objector, of silence."

The dumbness the witnesses read as a mercy as well as a chastisement. Chrysostom: "mark the mercy of God in what follows: Until the day in which these things shall be performed. As if he said, When by the issues of events I shall have proved my words... I will remove the punishment from you." The sign Zacharias had demanded (v.18) is given, but in the form of a chastisement that would itself confirm the word: his silence would last until the birth, and its removal (1:64) would attest the fulfilment.

The grammatical voices note the cause: "because thou believest not my words." The punishment was for unbelief, and was proportioned to it — the doubting tongue silenced until faith was vindicated by the event. Yet, as the witnesses note (with Calvin, under v.18), Zacharias's faith was not destroyed but only wavering; the chastisement was corrective, not condemning.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dumbness laid on Zacharias was both the sign he had demanded and the chastisement of his unbelief — a punishment fitted to his fault (the doubting tongue silenced), yet tempered with mercy, for it would last only until the birth, and its removal would attest the word's fulfilment. The priest who should have been an example of faith was the more smitten for his doubt; yet the chastisement was corrective, not condemning, his faith wavering but not destroyed. The verse displays the justice and mercy of God together: the unbelief justly punished, yet in a way that would itself confirm the promise and restore the chastened priest to speech and song (1:64, 67ff). The sign of silence both rebuked the doubt and sealed the certainty of the word, which would surely "be fulfilled in their season."
21:
"And the people waited for Zacharias, and marvelled that he tarried so long in the temple." The witnesses note the waiting congregation and their wonder at the priest's delay. The Greek Exposition: "while these things were going on within, the delay excited surprise among the multitudes who were waiting without... And while various suspicions were going about, each man repeating them as it pleased him, Zacharias coming forth told by his silence what he secretly endured." Gill and the grammatical voices explain that the people waited for the priest to come out and pronounce the priestly blessing (Numbers 6:23-26), and his unusual delay in the holy place caused them to marvel.

The delay the witnesses connect to the vision within. Zacharias tarried because he was conversing with the angel and then, struck dumb, was unable to perform his part. The people, who knew nothing of what passed within, could only wonder at the lengthening of the service beyond its custom. Their marvelling prepared them to perceive, when he came out unable to speak (v.22), that he had seen a vision.

The scene the witnesses (with Henry, under v.5 area) read as part of the public attestation of the event. The whole multitude was present and waiting; the priest's delay and his subsequent dumbness were witnessed by many, so that the extraordinary visitation, though seen by Zacharias alone, was attested by the public signs that followed. Lightfoot supplies the historical detail of the temple service and the expectation of the blessing.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the people, waiting without for the priest to emerge and bless them, marvelled at his unusual delay in the holy place — a delay caused by his conversation with the angel and his being struck dumb. Their wonder, knowing nothing of what passed within, prepared them to perceive that he had seen a vision when he came out speechless (v.22). The scene forms part of the public attestation of the event: the whole congregation present, the delay marked, the dumbness witnessed by many — so that the extraordinary visitation, though private in the holy place, was confirmed by public signs. The waiting people, expecting a blessing, received instead the silent witness of a priest who had met an angel; and their marvelling is the first ripple of the wonder that the births of John and Jesus would spread through the land. The hour of incense had become the hour of a sign.
22:
"And when he came out, he could not speak unto them: and they perceived that he had seen a vision in the temple: for he beckoned unto them, and remained speechless." The witnesses note the priest's speechless emergence and the people's perception. Theophylact: "Zacharias beckoned to the people, who perhaps inquired the cause of his silence, which, as he was not able to speak, he signified to them by nodding." Ambrose: "a nod is a certain action of the body, without speech endeavoring to declare the will, yet not expressing it." Gill and the grammatical voices note that the people inferred from his dumbness and his gestures that he had seen a vision in the holy place.

The people's perception the witnesses read as the first public attestation of the divine visitation. Unable to speak, Zacharias could only beckon and make signs; and the people, marvelling at his delay (v.21) and now seeing his speechlessness, rightly concluded that something supernatural had befallen him. The dumbness that was his chastisement (v.20) thus became also a public sign, confirming to the congregation that the priest had met with God.

The scene the witnesses note as the providential spreading of the news. Though Zacharias could not tell what he had seen, his very silence proclaimed it; and the people carried away the report that a vision had been granted in the temple. The dumb priest, beckoning to the people, is a striking picture of the word held back and yet attested — the message confirmed by the sign even when the messenger cannot speak.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Zacharias came out unable to speak, and the people, perceiving his speechlessness and his beckoning gestures, rightly concluded that he had seen a vision in the temple. The dumbness, his chastisement for unbelief (v.20), became also the public sign that attested the divine visitation — for his very silence, joined to his delay (v.21), proclaimed to the congregation that something supernatural had befallen him. The verse marks the spreading of the wonder: though the priest could not tell what he had seen, his silence told it, and the people carried away the news of a vision granted in the holy place. The dumb priest beckoning to the people is a vivid image of the word both restrained and confirmed — the message attested by the sign even when the tongue is bound. The births of John and Jesus would soon turn this first wonder into the song of salvation (1:64ff).
23:
"And it came to pass, that, as soon as the days of his ministration were accomplished, he departed to his own house." The witnesses note Zacharias's faithful completion of his service despite his dumbness. Bede, Origen, and Chrysostom (the fathers here) reflect on his perseverance in duty and his return home, where the promised conception would follow (vv.24-25). The grammatical voices explain that the course of Abia served a full week, and Zacharias, though struck dumb, remained to finish his appointed ministration before returning to his house in the hill country (v.39).

The faithful completion of his service the witnesses commend. Though chastised with dumbness, Zacharias did not abandon his post but continued in the priestly ministry until his week was accomplished. His perseverance in duty, even under chastisement, models the faithfulness that does not let personal affliction interrupt the service of God. Calvin and the grammatical voices note that he did not leave the temple prematurely but discharged his full course.

The return home the witnesses connect to the conception that follows. Having completed his ministration, Zacharias went to his own house, where Elizabeth conceived (v.24) — the fulfilment beginning of the angel's promise. The verse marks the transition from the temple scene to the domestic, from the announcement to its accomplishment.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Zacharias, though struck dumb, faithfully completed the days of his priestly ministration before returning home — a perseverance in duty under chastisement that models faithful service uninterrupted by personal affliction. Having finished his course, he departed to his own house, where the angel's promise would begin to be fulfilled in Elizabeth's conception (v.24). The verse marks the quiet completion of the temple scene and the transition to the home, where the wonder announced at the altar would take flesh. The dumb priest, faithfully discharging his office to the end, then returns to receive the blessing long prayed for and now granted. The fathers see in his perseverance and return the orderly unfolding of God's purpose, the promise moving from announcement toward accomplishment. (The Cyril of Jerusalem fragment here is a broader catechetical aside on the Church.)
24:
"And after those days his wife Elisabeth conceived, and hid herself five months, saying." The witnesses note the conception and Elizabeth's withdrawal. Gill and the grammatical voices explain that Elizabeth, having conceived according to the angel's word, hid herself for five months — whether from modesty, from a desire to give herself to private devotion and thanksgiving, or until her condition should be manifest. Lightfoot offers rabbinic background.

Elizabeth's retirement the witnesses read variously. Some take it as modesty or reverent privacy, giving herself to grateful meditation on God's mercy; others, that she concealed her condition until it was certain and evident, so that her later joy (v.25) and the visit of Mary (v.39) might confirm the wonder. Calvin and the grammatical voices note that her seclusion served the providential purpose of the narrative: when Mary came in the sixth month (v.36, v.39), Elizabeth's advanced condition would attest the truth of the angel's word to Mary.

The conception itself the witnesses note as the beginning of the fulfilment. The promise made at the altar (v.13) now took flesh; the long-barren Elizabeth conceived, and the forerunner's life began. The verse marks the first accomplishment of the angel's word, the wonder moving from announcement to reality.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Elizabeth conceived according to the angel's promise and hid herself five months — whether from modest reverence, giving herself to grateful devotion, or to let her condition become certain and manifest. Her seclusion served the unfolding of God's purpose: when Mary should come in the sixth month (vv.36, 39), Elizabeth's advanced condition would confirm the truth of the angel's word and the sign given to Mary. The conception itself, the first accomplishment of the promise made at the altar (v.13), marks the wonder passing from announcement to reality — the long-barren wife now bearing the forerunner. The verse, brief and quiet, records the beginning of the fulfilment; and Elizabeth's words (v.25) reveal the gratitude and joy with which she received the removal of her reproach. The hidden months would end in the open praise of God's mercy.
25:
"Thus hath the Lord dealt with me in the days wherein he looked on me, to take away my reproach among men." The witnesses note Elizabeth's grateful acknowledgment and the removal of her reproach. Gill and the grammatical voices explain that barrenness was esteemed a reproach among the Jews (as with Rachel, Genesis 30:23), a mark of divine displeasure or at least of withheld blessing; and Elizabeth, now conceiving, rejoices that God has looked on her and taken away that reproach.

Elizabeth's words the witnesses read as humble gratitude. She ascribes the conception wholly to the Lord — "thus hath the Lord dealt with me" — and recognizes it as the gracious notice of God, who "looked on me." Like Hannah (1 Samuel 1) and Rachel before her, she receives the gift of a child as the lifting of a reproach and the token of God's favour. Her gratitude models the right response to answered prayer: to acknowledge the hand of God and to glorify him for his mercy.

The removal of reproach the witnesses note as both personal and significant. Personally, Elizabeth is freed from the shame of barrenness; significantly, the conception of the forerunner is the beginning of the gospel age, in which the greater reproach of sin would be taken away by the Messiah whom John would announce. Calvin and the grammatical voices note the depth of Elizabeth's piety in recognizing God's hand.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the Zacharias narrative until the birth. The witnesses agree: Elizabeth, conceiving, gratefully acknowledges that the Lord has dealt with her and looked on her to take away her reproach — the shame of barrenness esteemed among the Jews a mark of withheld blessing. Her humble words ascribe the conception wholly to God's gracious notice, like Rachel and Hannah before her, and model the right response to answered prayer: to glorify the God who looked on her low estate. The removal of her reproach is both personal (freedom from the shame of childlessness) and significant (the forerunner's conception opening the gospel age, in which the deeper reproach of sin would be taken away by the Messiah). The verse displays the piety of Elizabeth and the mercy of God, who remembers the long-waiting and lifts the reproach of his people. The hidden months of gratitude prepare for the open joy of Mary's visit (v.39) and the song of salvation.
26:
"And in the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent from God unto a city of Galilee, named Nazareth." The witnesses note the timing, the messenger, and the place of the annunciation. The fathers read the sixth month and the sending richly. Bede: "rightly in the sixth month of John's conception was an angel sent to Mary, to tell her that a Savior should be born," and (a tradition) "our Lord is reported to have been conceived... on the twenty-fifth day of March... that with the increase of light He was conceived... Who lightens every man." He marks the symmetry of the fall and the restoration: "an angel should be sent down from God to consecrate a virgin by a divine birth, for the first cause of man's perdition was the Devil sending a serpent to deceive a woman." Gregory: "to the virgin Mary was sent, not any one of the angels, but the archangel Gabriel... as being the bearer of the highest of all tidings... Gabriel is interpreted, the strength of God."

The fathers also read the place. The Gloss: "He would come a Nazarite, (i.e. the holy of the holy)." Basil notes the angels visit "as the occasion conduces to our advantage." Chrysostom marks that the angel announced the birth before the conception, "standing by her in visible shape," not in a dream.

The puritan Henry gives the running exposition (covering vv.26-38): "the same angel, Gabriel, that was employed in making known to Zacharias God's purpose concerning his son, is employed in this also; for in this, the same glorious work of redemption... is carried on." He notes the lowliness of the place: Mary "lived in Nazareth, a city of Galilee, a remote corner of the country, and in no reputation."

The witnesses agree: in the sixth month of Elizabeth's pregnancy, the archangel Gabriel — "the strength of God," bearer of the highest tidings, and the same who had announced John's birth — was sent from God to lowly Nazareth in Galilee. The timing links the two annunciations, the forerunner's and the Messiah's; the sending of an archangel marks the surpassing greatness of the news; and the choice of an obscure town for so great an event displays the humility that would mark the whole work of redemption. The fathers read the symmetry of the angel sent to a virgin against the serpent sent to Eve — the restoration answering the fall. The verse opens the annunciation of the incarnation, the heart of the gospel, in the humble setting of a despised town.
27:
"To a virgin espoused to a man whose name was Joseph, of the house of David; and the virgin's name was Mary." The witnesses note the virgin, her espousal, and her Davidic connection. The fathers dwell on the providence of the espousal. Ambrose: "Scripture has rightly mentioned that she was espoused, as well as a virgin... that she might not be branded with the disgrace of sullied virginity... The Lord had rather that men should cast a doubt upon His birth than upon His mother's purity." He gives several reasons: the espousal shielded Mary's reputation, gave a witness (Joseph) to her purity, fulfilled the law (lest Christ "should seem to have had his beginning from a violation of the law"), and "baffled the prince of the world, who, when he perceived her espoused to a man, could cast no suspicion on her offspring." Origen: "if she had had no husband, soon would the thought have stolen into the Devil's mind, how she who had known no man could be pregnant."

On the Davidic descent, Bede notes that "of the house of David" applies "not only to Joseph, but also to Mary, for the Law commanded that every one should take a wife out of his own tribe or family." The grammatical voices (Robertson, Ellicott) discuss whether Mary herself was of David's line, which the promise of the Davidic throne (v.32) would require.

The puritan Henry (under v.26) draws out Mary's character: "She was a daughter of the royal family, lineally descended from David... though she was poor and low in the world." Her virginity was necessary "because he was not to be born by ordinary generation, but miraculously... that, though he must partake of the nature of man, yet not of the corruption of that nature." Yet she was espoused, "to put honour upon the married state."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the annunciation came to Mary, a virgin espoused to Joseph of the house of David. The espousal, the fathers note, was providential — shielding Mary's reputation, providing a witness to her purity, fulfilling the law, and baffling the devil, who could cast no suspicion on the offspring of an espoused woman. The Davidic connection (of both Joseph and, the witnesses hold, Mary) prepared for the promise of David's throne (v.32). Mary's virginity was necessary for the miraculous, sinless birth of the Redeemer; her espousal honoured the married state. The verse introduces the mother of the Lord — a lowly virgin of the royal line, espoused yet undefiled — in whom the incarnation would be wrought. The providence that joined virginity and espousal both guarded her honour and prepared the way for the birth of the sinless Son of God.
28:
"And the angel came in unto her, and said, Hail, thou that art highly favoured, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women." The witnesses note the angelic salutation and debate the rendering of "highly favoured." The grammatical voices weigh the Greek. Robertson: "Highly favoured (kecharitomene). Perfect passive participle of charitoo and means endowed with grace, enriched with grace as in Ephesians 1:6. The Vulgate is right, if it means full of grace; wrong, if it means full of grace [in the sense of a fountain of grace] (Plummer)." Ellicott: "the plena gratia of the Vulgate has no warrant in the meaning of the word." The phrase means one who has received grace, not one who dispenses it.

The Reformed Calvin makes this the ground of a sharp polemic against Mariolatry. The participle "denotes the undeserved favor of God... if Mary's happiness, righteousness, and life, flow from the undeserved love of God... it is the height of absurdity to tell us that we should seek from her what she derives from another quarter in the same manner as ourselves. With extraordinary ignorance have the Papists, by an enchanter's trick, changed this salutation into a prayer." Wesley agrees: "This salutation gives no room for any pretence of paying adoration to the virgin; as having no appearance of a prayer, or of worship offered to her," noting the same words were used of Noah, Gideon, and Jael.

The fathers read the salutation as the announcement of grace and joy. Gregory of Nyssa: "the angel not unaptly proclaims joy to the Virgin, saying, Hail" — far different from the sorrow announced to Eve. The Greek Exposition and Jerome: "Full of grace, for to others, grace comes in part; into Mary at once the fullness of grace wholly infused itself." On "the Lord is with thee," Augustine: "He Himself is in your heart, He is made in your womb." (The grammatical voices note "Blessed art thou among women" is omitted in the best manuscripts here, appearing in v.42.)

The witnesses agree on the substance — the angel salutes Mary as one signally favoured by God, with whom the Lord is — while differing sharply on the inferences. The Reformed and Wesleyan voices insist the word means "highly favoured" (one who has received grace), not "full of grace" (a fountain of grace), and reject the Roman use of the salutation as a prayer or warrant for adoration: Mary's favour flows from God's undeserved love, the same source from which believers draw. The fathers read the salutation as the announcement of grace and joy reversing Eve's sorrow. The verse marks the high honour put on Mary — chosen to be the mother of the Redeemer — while the witnesses guard against turning that honour into worship.
29:
"And when she saw him, she was troubled at his saying, and cast in her mind what manner of salutation this should be." The witnesses note Mary's reaction — troubled, yet thoughtful. The grammatical voices mark the difference between her and Zacharias. Ellicott: "The same word is used as had been used of Zacharias. With Mary, as with him, the first feeling was one of natural terror." Robertson: "She was both upset and puzzled." Yet the Greek Exposition (under v.28) had noted "the modesty and wisdom of the Virgin... When she heard the joyful words, she pondered them in her mind, and neither openly resisted through unbelief, nor forthwith lightly complied; avoiding equally the inconstancy of Eve, and the insensibility of Zacharias."

Mary's being troubled "at his saying" rather than at the vision the witnesses note. The Greek Exposition: "as she might be accustomed to these visions, the Evangelist ascribes her agitation not to the vision, but to the things told her." It was the strangeness of the salutation — the high honour and grace ascribed to her — that troubled and perplexed her, not merely the appearance of the angel.

The Reformed Calvin reads her trouble as reverent awe: "Perceiving in the angel something of heavenly glory, she was seized with sudden dread arising out of reverence for God." Yet her composure appears in her pondering: "Luke does not say that she was so terrified as to have lost recollection. On the contrary, he mentions an indication of an attentive and composed mind." Lightfoot notes how rare and unusual such a salutation to a woman was.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Mary was troubled at the angel's salutation and pondered its meaning — troubled not merely at the vision but at the strange, high honour and grace ascribed to her, yet composed enough to weigh its meaning in her mind. Her reaction displays both modesty and wisdom: she neither resisted in unbelief (like Eve) nor complied lightly (the Greek Exposition), but reverently considered what manner of greeting this could be. Her trouble was the awe of reverence for God, not the doubt of unbelief; and her pondering marks the attentive, thoughtful spirit that would shortly yield in faith (v.38). The verse contrasts Mary's reverent perplexity with Zacharias's doubting demand for a sign, and prepares for the angel's reassurance (v.30). The virgin who weighed the salutation in her heart is the same who would keep all these things and ponder them (2:19) — a model of faithful, meditative reception of God's word.
30:
"And the angel said unto her, Fear not, Mary: for thou hast found favour with God." The witnesses note the angel's reassurance and the meaning of "favour." Chrysostom: "he who earns favor in the sight of God has nothing to fear... how shall any one find it, except through the means of his humility. For God gives grace to the humble." The Greek Exposition: "the Virgin found favor with God, in that decking her own soul in the bright robes of chastity, she prepared a dwelling-place pleasing to God... she kept [her conscience] from stain."

On "favour," the grammatical voices note it is the word "grace." Robertson: "Grace. Same root as chairo (rejoice) and charitoo in verse 28. To find favour is a common O.T. phrase... The notion of kindness is in it also, especially of God towards men as here." Ellicott prefers "favour" here to avoid the technical theological associations of "grace." Barnes: "Eminent favour or mercy in being selected to be the mother of the Messiah."

The Reformed Calvin reads the reassurance and the idiom: "He bids her lay aside fear... whenever the feeblest ray of the Divine glory bursts upon us, we cannot avoid being alarmed." On "found favour": "a person is said to find favour, not when he has sought it, but when it has been freely offered to him." The favour is undeserved and free, the gracious choice of God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel stills Mary's fear with the assurance that she has found favour with God — undeserved, freely-given grace, the gracious choice of God to make her the mother of the Messiah. The "favour" (grace) is the same word used in the salutation (v.28): Mary is one who has received God's free kindness, not earned it. The fathers note that the humble and chaste find such favour, Mary having prepared in her soul a dwelling pleasing to God; yet Calvin guards that favour is found not by seeking but by its being freely offered. The verse turns Mary's reverent trouble (v.29) to peace, as the angel assures her of God's gracious choice, and prepares for the announcement of the conception (v.31). The "Fear not" to Mary, like that to Zacharias (v.13), is the word of grace that opens the way for the message of salvation. The favoured virgin is the freely-chosen vessel of the incarnation.
31:
"And, behold, thou shalt conceive in thy womb, and bring forth a son, and shalt call his name JESUS." The witnesses note the announcement of the conception and the meaning of the name. The grammatical voices connect it to prophecy. Robertson: "Conceive in thy womb. Same idiom in Isaiah 7:14 of Immanuel." Ellicott notes Mary understood the words "in the sense which St. Matthew gives to those of the prophet [Isaiah 7:14]... What perplexed her was the reference to the conception and the birth in a prediction which made no mention of her approaching marriage."

On the name Jesus, the witnesses unfold its meaning. Calvin: the name "is derived from the Hebrew word salvation... And so the name contains a promise of salvation, and points out the object for which he was sent by the Father into the world." He notes it was "the same name with Joshua," but "what was exhibited in shadow under the law is fully and actually manifested in the Son of God." Cyril of Jerusalem: "Jesus in Hebrew is by interpretation Saviour... He was openly called Jesus not by men, but by an Angel... for He shall save His people from their sins."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine against the heretics: "The word bring forth is enough to set aside the dream of Marcion and Manichaeus: for it is easy to gather from it that Mary brought forth not an ethereal body or phantom, but the fruit which she had previously conceived in her womb." The real conception and birth establish the true humanity of Christ.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel announces that Mary shall conceive and bring forth a son named Jesus — "Saviour" — the very child foretold by Isaiah (7:14), conceived really in her womb and born of her substance. The name Jesus, given by the angel and not by men, contains the promise of salvation and points to the purpose of his coming: "he shall save his people from their sins." The real conception and birth, the witnesses note, establish the true humanity of Christ against those who would make his body a phantom. The verse announces the incarnation of the Saviour — true man, conceived and born of the virgin — and names him by the name that is itself the gospel: Jesus, who saves. The same name borne in shadow by Joshua is now fulfilled in substance by the Son of God, the Saviour of his people.
32:
"He shall be great, and shall be called the Son of the Highest: and the Lord God shall give unto him the throne of his father David." The witnesses note the greatness, the divine sonship, and the Davidic throne. Gill: "great in his person, as God-man... in his offices... in his works... and in the glory he is now possessed of." The grammatical voices note the language is drawn from prophecy. Barnes: "There is undoubted reference in this passage to Isaiah 9:6-7." Robertson: "The language of 2 Samuel 7:14; Isaiah 9:7 is combined here."

On "Son of the Highest," the witnesses distinguish Christ's sonship from John's greatness. Calvin: "The angel had said the same thing about John the Baptist [great], and yet did not intend to make him equal to Christ... to him alone this belongs as his own peculiar prerogative to be called the Son of God." He cites Hebrews 1:5 and Psalm 2:7: "Christ is not confounded either with angels or with men... We too are sons, but by adoption... Christ is the only Son, the only-begotten of the Father (John 1:14)." Bede: "Let Nestorius then cease to say that the Virgin's Son is only man... the Angel demonstrates the one Person of Christ in two natures."

On "the throne of David," the witnesses read it of the spiritual kingdom. Ellicott: "The form of the promise, which might well lead to the expectation of a revived kingdom of Israel... takes its place among the most memorable instances of prophecies that have been fulfilled in quite another fashion than those who first heard them could have imagined." Basil: "The seat of David is that on which our Lord reestablished His spiritual kingdom which should never be destroyed." Cyril of Jerusalem reads the throne not as wood but as "the kingdom itself."

The witnesses agree: the angel announces that Mary's son shall be great — not merely as John was great, but as the only-begotten Son of the Highest, his own peculiar prerogative — and shall receive the throne of his father David. The language, drawn from Isaiah 9 and 2 Samuel 7, combines the divine sonship and the Davidic kingship in the one person of Christ, true God and true man (against Nestorius). The throne of David is fulfilled not in a revived earthly kingdom but in the spiritual, everlasting kingdom of the Messiah. The verse proclaims the deity and royalty of the coming child: the Son of God who is also the Son of David, the eternal King whose kingdom shall have no end (v.33).
33:
"And he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there shall be no end." The witnesses note the everlasting, universal kingdom of Christ. Gill reads "the house of Jacob" spiritually: "not over the Jews, the posterity of Jacob, in a literal sense; but over the whole Israel of God, consisting of Jews and Gentiles."
