Barnes: "the children of Israel... it is the same as saying that he would reign over his own church and people for ever." Robertson notes Luke records "the perpetuity of this Davidic kingdom over the house of Jacob with no Pauline interpretation of the spiritual Israel though that was the true meaning."

The Reformed Calvin explains how the kingdom, fixed first in Israel, extends to the world: "Christ's throne was erected among the people of Israel, that he might thence subdue the whole world. All whom he has joined by faith to the children of Abraham are accounted the true Israel." He notes the Jews' revolt but God's preserved remnant (Romans 11:5), and that "the church, which is scattered through the whole world, is the spiritual Israel, for it drew its origin from Zion."

On "no end," the witnesses read the everlasting kingdom. Calvin: the angel employs "the words of Daniel (7:14)... God will for ever protect and defend the kingdom of Christ and the church... yet its true perpetuity relates to the glory to come." Ellicott addresses 1 Corinthians 15:24-28: the mediatorial kingdom "ceases by being perfected, not by passing away after the fashion of earthly kingdoms. The delegated or mediatorial headship of the Christ is merged in the absolute unity of the monarchy of God."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the angel's announcement of the kingdom. The witnesses agree: Christ shall reign for ever over the house of Jacob — the spiritual Israel of Jews and Gentiles joined by faith — and his kingdom shall have no end. The Davidic throne (v.32) is fulfilled in the everlasting, universal kingdom of the Messiah, fixed first in Israel but extending through faith to the whole world. The "house of Jacob" is the church, the true Israel drawn from Zion; the "no end" is the eternal perpetuity of Christ's reign, drawn from Daniel 7:14. The mediatorial kingdom, the witnesses note (Ellicott), is not abolished but perfected, merged at last in the unity of God's eternal monarchy. The verse crowns the annunciation of the Messiah's royalty: the Son of David who is the Son of God reigns for ever, and his kingdom — the everlasting communion of his redeemed people — shall never pass away. The eternal King is announced to the lowly virgin.
34:
"Then said Mary unto the angel, How shall this be, seeing I know not a man?" The witnesses contrast Mary's question of faith with Zacharias's doubt, and reject the notion of a vow of perpetual virginity. Ambrose marks the difference: "How subdued her answer is, compared with the words of the Priest... He refuses to believe that which he says he does not know... She avows herself willing to do that which she doubts not will be done, but how, she is anxious to know. Mary had read, Behold, she shall conceive and bear a son. She believed therefore that it should be; but how it was to take place she had never read." Ellicott: "Mary is told of a far greater marvel... she, accepting the words in faith, does not demand a sign, but reverently seeks to know the manner of their accomplishment."

Gregory of Nyssa reads her words as the token of her chastity: "she rests upon her virginity, deeming her inviolability a more precious thing than the Angel's declaration." Gill: "she did not dispute it, but wanted to be resolved by what means it would be brought about: she knew, by prophecy, that the Messiah was to be born of a virgin."

The Reformed Calvin both grants Mary a measure of weakness and defends her against the Roman gloss: "She ought immediately to have risen by faith to the boundless power of God... Instead of this, she stops at the ordinary way of generation. Still, it must be admitted that she does not hesitate or inquire in such a manner as to lower the power of God." He sharply rejects the vow-of-virginity reading: "The conjecture which some have drawn from these words, that she had formed a vow of perpetual virginity, is unfounded and altogether absurd. She would, in that case, have committed treachery by allowing herself to be united to a husband... it is an idle and unfounded supposition that a monastic life existed among the Jews."

The witnesses agree: Mary's question — "How shall this be?" — was the inquiry of faith, not the doubt of unbelief. Unlike Zacharias, who disbelieved the fact and demanded a sign, Mary believed the fact (knowing the Messiah was to be virgin-born) and reverently asked only the manner of its accomplishment. Her words rest on her virginity, which she esteemed precious; but they imply no vow of perpetual virginity (which Calvin rejects as absurd and unfounded), only her present untouched state. The verse contrasts believing inquiry with doubting demand, and displays Mary's faith and chastity together — the model of a soul that accepts God's word and humbly seeks to understand his way.
35:
"And the angel answered and said unto her, The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee: therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God." The witnesses note the manner of the virgin conception and the two natures of Christ. The grammatical voices explain the imagery. Robertson: "Shall overshadow thee. A figure of a cloud coming upon her... like the Shekinah glory which suggests it (Exodus 40:38) where the cloud of glory represents the presence and power of God." Ellicott connects it to the Spirit's quickening work (Genesis 1:2; Psalm 104:30). Barnes: "the body of Jesus would be created by the direct power of God... it was necessary that his human nature should be pure, and free from the corruption of the fall."

The fathers and Reformed read the conception as sinless and the child as truly God and man. Gregory of Nyssa: "the true Lawgiver has formed anew to Himself from our dust without cohabitation, creating a body capable of taking His divinity." Cyril of Jerusalem: "Immaculate and undefiled was His generation: for where the Holy Spirit breathes, there all pollution is taken away." Calvin: Christ "must be a real man, in order that he might expiate our sins... so was it necessary, in order to his cleansing others, that he should be free from every spot and blemish (1 Peter 1:19)... The Spirit of God kept him pure from the very commencement."

On "shall be called the Son of God," a genuine debate arises over its bearing on Christ's eternal Sonship. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke launches a striking polemic against the doctrine of the "eternal Sonship": "the doctrine of the eternal Sonship of Christ is, in my opinion, anti-scriptural, and highly dangerous... Son supposes time, generation, and father... the phrase eternal Son is a positive self-contradiction." He holds the title "Son of God" rests on the supernatural birth. Wesley, by contrast, affirms both: "not only as he is God from eternity, but on this account likewise he shall be called the Son of God." Calvin defends the eternal Sonship against Servetus: "he who had been the Son of God from eternity, appeared also as the Son of God in human flesh... As the name Son belonged to the divine essence of Christ from the beginning, so now it is applied unitedly to both natures."

The witnesses agree on the substance — the virgin conception wrought by the overshadowing Holy Spirit, producing a holy, sinless child who is the Son of God — while differing on whether the title "Son of God" here rests on the supernatural birth alone (Clarke) or also on his eternal generation (Calvin, Wesley). All affirm the two natures: true man (of Mary's substance, that he might expiate sin) and truly God (the Son of the Highest). The verse is the Christological heart of the annunciation: the incarnation of the eternal Son in a sinless human nature, conceived by the Spirit's power, that the Saviour might be both God and man.
36:
"And, behold, thy cousin Elisabeth, she hath also conceived a son in her old age: and this is the sixth month with her, who was called barren." The witnesses note the confirming sign given to Mary and the union of priestly and royal lines. Chrysostom: "Seeing that his previous words had overcome the mind of the virgin, the angel drops his discourse to a humbler subject, persuading her by reference to sensible things... he brings forward a recent event, that he might the more forcibly strike her mind." Bede: "lest the virgin should despair of being able to bear a son, she received the example of one both old and barren about to bring forth, in order that she might learn that all things are possible with God."

The kinship of Mary and Elizabeth the witnesses discuss, since one was of David (Judah) and the other of Aaron (Levi). Gregory of Nazianzus (via the Catena): "this was brought about by the Divine counsel, to the end that the royal race might be united to the priestly stock; that Christ, Who is both King and Priest, might be descended from both." Calvin and the grammatical voices explain the law permitting such intermarriage: "if any woman of the tribe of Judah married a priest... No such danger existed [of removing inheritances]"; so "the mother of the holy virgin might be descended from the family of Aaron."

The sign the witnesses read as God's gracious condescension to Mary's weakness. Calvin: "by an instance taken from her own relatives, the angel encourages the faith of Mary to expect a miracle... With equal indulgence does he cheer and support us every day; nay, with greater indulgence, because our faith is weaker." Lightfoot: "the angel teaches to what purpose it was that women, either barren before or considerably stricken in years, should be enabled to conceive... viz. to make way for the easier belief of the conception of a virgin."

The witnesses agree: the angel offers Mary a confirming sign — the conception of her aged kinswoman Elizabeth, six months along — that her faith might be strengthened to expect the greater miracle. The example of the barren and old conceiving makes way for belief in the virgin's conception, teaching that all things are possible with God. The kinship of Mary (of David) and Elizabeth (of Aaron) unites the royal and priestly lines, fitting for Christ who is both King and Priest. The verse displays God's gracious condescension to the weakness of faith: he supports his servants with confirming signs, the more indulgently as their faith is weaker. The sign of Elizabeth both encourages Mary and links the two annunciations, preparing for her visit to her kinswoman (v.39).
37:
"For with God nothing shall be impossible." The witnesses note the ground of all the angel's promises — the omnipotence of God. Gill: "every thing that he has said, purposed, or promised, he is able to do, and will... particularly this of a virgin's conceiving and bearing a Son." Clarke connects it to the promise to Sarah: "Words of the very same import with those spoken by the Lord to Sarah, when he foretold the birth of Isaac, Genesis 18:14, Is any thing too hard for the Lord? As there can be no doubt that Mary perceived this allusion... she must have had her faith considerably strengthened."

The grammatical voices note the precise word. Robertson: "No word (ouk rhema). Rhema brings out the single item rather than the whole content (logos)." The promise is that no word or thing spoken by God shall be impossible of fulfilment.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the right use of God's power: "we ought always to hold it as a maxim, that they wander widely from the truth who, at their pleasure, imagine the power of God to be something beyond his word." He guards against idle speculation: "it is idle, and unprofitable, and even dangerous, to argue what God can do unless we also take into account what he resolves to do. The angel does here what God frequently does in Scripture, employs a general doctrine to confirm one kind of promise." And he notes that "true faith apprehends the power of God, not in the air, but with its results" — that is, joined to his word and its accomplishment.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of all the angel's promises is the omnipotence of God — "with God nothing shall be impossible." The same word spoken to Sarah at the promise of Isaac (Genesis 18:14, "Is anything too hard for the Lord?") now confirms the greater wonder of the virgin's conception, and Mary, perceiving the allusion, has her faith strengthened. Calvin teaches the right use of this doctrine: not idle speculation about what God could do, but confidence joined to his word — true faith apprehends his power not "in the air" but in its results, as the certain accomplishment of what he has promised. The verse anchors the whole annunciation in the boundless power of God, against which no promise is impossible. The God who made the barren conceive and the virgin bear is able to perform all he has spoken; and faith, resting on his power joined to his word, may believe the incredible. With God, the word once spoken cannot fail.
38:
"And Mary said, Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word. And the angel departed from her." The witnesses note Mary's faith and submission, the climax of the annunciation. Ambrose: "Behold now the humility, the devotion of the virgin... She calls herself His handmaid, who is chosen to be His mother, so far was she from being exalted by the sudden promise... Behold the handmaid of the Lord, signifies the readiness of duty. Be it to me according to your word, the conception of the wish." The Greek Exposition praises "the depth of her humility."

The Reformed Calvin makes her words the model of faith: "The holy virgin does not allow herself to dispute any farther... She stops the entrance of opposing arguments, and compels herself to obey. This is the real proof of faith, when we restrain our minds, and, as it were, hold them captive, so that they dare not reply this or that to God: for boldness in disputing, on the other hand, is the mother of unbelief." He draws the contrast: "as faith alone makes us obedient servants to God, and gives us up to his power, so unbelief makes us rebels and deserters."

The grammatical voices note the cost Mary accepts. Ellicott: "The words seem to show a kind of half-consciousness that the lot which she thus accepts might bring with it unknown sufferings, as well as untold blessedness. She shrinks, as it were, from the awfulness of the position... but she can say, as her Son said afterwards... Not my will, but Thine be done." Barnes: "an expression of resignation to the will of God, and of faith in the promise."

The witnesses agree: Mary's response — "Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word" — is the climax of the annunciation and the model of faith and submission. Calling herself the Lord's handmaid, she neither disputes nor delays but surrenders herself wholly to God's will, holding her mind captive to his word — the very proof of faith, as unbelief is the mother of disputing. Her submission, perhaps half-conscious of the suffering as well as the blessedness it would bring, anticipates her Son's "Not my will, but Thine be done." The verse displays the humility and faith of the mother of the Lord: chosen to the highest honour, she claims only the place of a servant, and yields herself to God's purpose. The angel departs, his message received in faith; and the virgin who said "be it unto me" becomes the willing vessel of the incarnation. Her faith stands forever as the pattern of glad, obedient surrender to the word of God.
39:
"And Mary arose in those days, and went into the hill country with haste, into a city of Juda." The witnesses note Mary's prompt journey to Elizabeth and its motives. Ambrose: "rejoicing in the fulfillment of her wish, and conscientious in the observance of her duty, she gladly went forth into the hill country. For what could Mary now, filled with God, but ascend into the higher parts with haste!" Origen reads the haste spiritually: "Jesus who was in her womb hastened to sanctify John, still in the womb of his mother." Ambrose: "The grace of the Holy Spirit knows not of slow workings."

The fathers draw lessons from Mary's journey. Ambrose: "Learn, O holy women, the attention which you ought to show for your kinswomen with child... Learn also, O virgins, the lowliness of Mary. She came a kinswoman to her next of kin, the younger to the elder, nor did she merely come to her, but was the first to give her salutations... For the more chaste a virgin is, the more humble she should be." He sees in the visit "a cause of piety, in that the higher went to the lower, that the lower might be assisted, Mary to Elisabeth, Christ to John."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.39-45) reads the visit as ordered by providence. Mary went to Elizabeth, the sign the angel had given her (v.36), partly to confirm her faith, partly for the mutual comfort of the two favoured women. The grammatical voices note the "hill country" was the region of Judah where Zacharias dwelt, a journey of some distance from Nazareth, undertaken promptly.

The witnesses agree: Mary, filled with God and prompted by faith, journeyed promptly to the hill country of Judah to visit Elizabeth — the sign the angel had given her. Her haste displays the eagerness of grace, which knows no slow workings; her visit, the humility of the chosen mother, who, though the higher, went to the lower and was the first to greet her elder kinswoman. The fathers read in the journey both a pattern of humility and charity (the younger serving the elder, the higher the lower) and a mystery (Christ in the womb hastening to sanctify John). The verse opens the visitation, in which the two favoured women, and the two unborn children, would meet — Elizabeth's babe leaping for joy (v.41) at the coming of the Lord. Mary's prompt, humble journey is the response of faith, which moves at once to confirm the word and to share the joy of God's mercy.
40:
"And entered into the house of Zacharias, and saluted Elisabeth." The brief verse — with Gill, Barnes, and Robertson — records Mary's entrance and greeting. Gill notes she came to "the house of Zacharias," in the hill country of Judah, and greeted Elizabeth her kinswoman. Robertson observes the simple narrative of the meeting of the two favoured women.

Mary's salutation the witnesses (with Ambrose, under v.39) read as a mark of humility: the younger and (in worldly terms) lesser-known went first to greet the elder, "nor did she merely come to her, but was the first to give her salutations." The chosen mother of the Lord, though bearing the greater honour, takes the place of the servant, greeting her elder kinswoman with reverence and love.

The greeting also sets in motion the wonder that follows (v.41): at the sound of Mary's salutation, the babe leaped in Elizabeth's womb, and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Ghost. The Greek Exposition (under v.39) had noted "the babe leaped... which was the greatest token of his joy. Who ever heard of leaping at a time previous to birth? Grace introduced things to which nature was a stranger." Mary's simple greeting was the occasion of the forerunner's first witness to his Lord.

The thin comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: Mary entered the house of Zacharias and greeted Elizabeth — a simple act that both displays her humility (the chosen mother greeting first her elder kinswoman) and sets in motion the wonder of the visitation. The salutation of Mary, who bore the Lord in her womb, was the occasion of the babe John's leaping for joy and of Elizabeth's Spirit-filled blessing (vv.41-42) — the forerunner's first witness to the Christ, even before birth. The verse closes the opening section of the chapter, the two annunciations now meeting in the encounter of the two favoured women. Mary's humble greeting, the response of faith that moved at once to her kinswoman (v.39), opens the joyful scene in which the unborn forerunner and the unborn Messiah are brought together, and in which Elizabeth's blessing and Mary's Magnificat (vv.42-55) would pour forth the praise of God's mercy. The visit of faith bears the fruit of joy.
41:
‘The babe leaped in her womb; and Elisabeth was filled with the Holy Ghost.’ The witnesses note the unborn forerunner’s exultation and the prophetic filling of his mother.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the event. Robertson notes the leaping was ‘a common enough incident with unborn children’ (Genesis 25:22), but that Elisabeth was ‘filled with the Holy Spirit to understand what had happened to Mary.’ Lightfoot cites the rabbinic notion of infants exulting in the womb. Ellicott notes that the account, ‘so far as we look to human sources, must obviously have come to St. Luke… from the Virgin herself.’

The Wesleyan Clarke sees here the fulfilment of the angel’s promise that John should be ‘filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother’s womb’ (v.15). The Reformed Calvin, cautious, holds the ‘extraordinary occurrence’ lay chiefly in the mother, suddenly endued with the Spirit, rather than in any conscious piety of the embryo.

The shared, dominant note is that the meeting of the two mothers was marked by a movement of the Spirit: the unborn John stirred, and Elisabeth was filled with prophetic insight to greet Mary as the mother of her Lord. The witnesses divide only on whether the babe’s leaping was itself a sign of his recognising Christ or, with Calvin, a token wrought in the mother; but all agree the scene displays the early sanctifying of the forerunner and the Spirit’s witness, before any word was spoken, to the One whom Mary bore. Barnes notes the filling with the Holy Ghost meant Elisabeth was endued with joy, a disposition to praise, and a prophetic knowledge of the child Mary bore; the witnesses agree the whole scene, before a word was spoken, displays the Spirit’s witness to the One Mary carried, and the early sanctifying of the forerunner who, even unborn, exulted at the nearness of his Lord. Lightfoot likens the leaping to that of the children in Rebekah’s womb (Genesis 25:22) and the skipping of the mountains, a vehement exultation; the witnesses see in it the Spirit’s work both in the babe and in the mother who read the sign aright.
42:
‘Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb.’ The witnesses note Elisabeth’s Spirit-prompted benediction of Mary and her child.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Robertson notes the ‘loud cry’ of ‘ecstatic excitement,’ the perfect participle ‘blessed’ being a Hebraistic superlative. Barnes and Clarke note Elisabeth repeats the angel’s greeting (v.28), of which Mary had probably told her. Ellicott marks the contrast with the same words applied to Jael (Judges 5:24).

The Reformed Calvin guards the balance: Elisabeth does not put Mary and Christ ‘on an equal footing,’ but the second clause assigns the reason — Mary is blessed ‘on account of the blessedness of her child’; yet to bear Christ in the womb was ‘greatly inferior to’ being ‘born again by the Spirit.’ The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here treats Philip in Samaria, tangential.

The shared, dominant note is that Elisabeth, filled with the Spirit, pronounces Mary blessed among women and her child blessed — the blessing of the mother flowing from the blessedness of the Son. The witnesses agree the benediction is genuine honour, but ordered aright: Mary is highly favoured for the sake of the fruit of her womb, the Lord she carries. Calvin’s caution stands — that her deeper blessedness lay not in the physical bearing of Christ but in her faith and spiritual renewal — so that the verse honours Mary without dishonouring God by overpraise. Barnes notes Elisabeth esteemed it the highest honour among mothers to be the mother of the Messiah, repeating the angel’s words; the witnesses agree the benediction, prompted by the Spirit, honours Mary truly yet rightly, the blessing of the mother flowing from the blessedness of the Son she bore, so that God is glorified and not dishonoured by overpraise of the handmaid. Ellicott notes the singular contrast between these words applied to the murderess Jael (Judges 5:24) and here to the mother of the Lord; the witnesses see in the Spirit-prompted greeting a true but ordered honour, Mary blessed for the sake of the blessed fruit of her womb.
43:
‘And whence is this to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?’ The witnesses note Elisabeth’s humility and her Spirit-given recognition of the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the confession. Barnes and Ellicott note ‘the mother of my Lord’ uses ‘Lord’ in its highest sense (Psalms 110:1): great as her own son was to be, here was the mother of one yet greater, ‘even of the Lord himself.’ Robertson notes only the Holy Spirit could reveal to Elisabeth that Mary was to be the Messiah’s mother. Clarke marks the prophetic spirit that gave her this insight.

The Reformed Calvin praises the ‘happy medium’ Elisabeth observes — valuing God’s gifts in Mary without idolising them, avoiding both envy and superstition. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on point: ‘the Holy Spirit wrought in Elisabeth… and that noble hand-maiden says of her own Lord, And whence is this to me?’

The shared, dominant note is that Elisabeth, the elder and the priest’s wife, humbly wonders that the mother of her Lord should visit her. The witnesses agree this is both a model of humility — counting the visit an undeserved honour — and a Spirit-taught confession of the deity of the child Mary bore, naming him ‘my Lord’ before his birth. Calvin’s balance is the lesson: to honour rightly the gifts God bestows on others, neither despising them through envy nor exalting them into idols. Clarke marks that the prophetic spirit overshadowing Elisabeth gave her a clear understanding of the mystery of the Messiah’s birth; the witnesses agree her wondering humility — that the mother of her Lord should visit her — is the pattern of a heart that counts God’s favours an undeserved honour, and her naming of the unborn child ‘my Lord’ a Spirit-taught confession of his deity. Ellicott leaves no doubt the word ‘Lord’ is used in its highest sense (Psalms 110:1), here was the mother of one greater than her own great son; the witnesses see in the elder Elisabeth’s wonder a model of humility before the grace of God.
44:
‘For lo… the babe leaped in my womb for joy.’ Only one voice comments, so the synthesis is brief; Gill notes Elisabeth names the babe’s leaping as her sign.

The Reformed Gill explains that Elisabeth mentions this ‘as the signal by which she knew that she was the mother of her Lord’ — namely, from that ‘unusual and extraordinary motion of the child she felt within her.’

With only one brief voice the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Elisabeth tells Mary the ground of her insight: at the sound of Mary’s greeting the unborn John leaped, and not merely in the natural way, but ‘for joy.’ The leap, as Lightfoot observed on the previous verse, signifies the manner of the thing — a vehement exultation — rather than implying full understanding in the embryo.

The shared, dominant note, consistent with the surrounding verses, is that the babe’s joyful stirring was the Spirit’s sign to Elisabeth of the presence of her Lord. The forerunner, even before birth, exulted at the nearness of the Christ; and his mother, reading the sign aright by the Spirit, greeted Mary as the mother of her Lord. The witnesses see in the leaping ‘for joy’ an early and fitting token of John’s whole office — to go before the face of the Lord and to rejoice at his coming, the friend of the Bridegroom glad even in the womb at the Bridegroom’s approach. Barnes and the grammatical commentators note Elisabeth grounds her insight in the babe’s leaping, the Spirit using the natural motion as a sign; the witnesses agree the forerunner, glad even in the womb at the Bridegroom’s approach, here begins his lifelong office of rejoicing before the face of the Lord and pointing others to him. Gill notes Elisabeth points to the babe’s leaping as the very signal by which she knew Mary to be the mother of her Lord; the witnesses agree the forerunner’s joy, before his birth, fittingly opens his whole office of going before the Lord and rejoicing at his coming. The leap, as Lightfoot observed, signifies the manner of the thing — a vehement exultation — rather than implying full understanding in the embryo.
45:
‘And blessed is she that believed: for there shall be a performance of those things which were told her from the Lord.’ The witnesses note the first beatitude of the New Testament, pronounced on Mary’s faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the words. Robertson calls this ‘the first beatitude in the New Testament,’ akin to the last spoken to Thomas (John 20:29), and notes the Greek hoti may be ‘that’ or ‘because.’ Ellicott and Clarke note the two renderings — ‘blessed… for there shall be’ or ‘blessed that there shall be’ — the latter perhaps avoiding the sense that the fulfilment hung on her faith. Barnes draws lessons on the blessedness of believing God’s promises.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin contrast Mary’s faith with the unbelief of Zacharias: a young virgin believed an incredible thing without a sign, where the aged priest had doubted a lesser one; Wesley too marks the tacit contrast. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on God forming man in the womb, tangential.

The shared, dominant note is that Elisabeth pronounces Mary blessed for her faith — the New Testament’s first beatitude. The witnesses agree faith is here set forth as the ground of true happiness, since it receives the fulfilment of God’s promises; and that Mary’s ready believing, contrasted with Zacharias’s doubt, is honoured as the pattern of the trust that takes God at his word. Whether read ‘for’ or ‘that,’ the verse joins her blessedness to her belief that what the Lord had spoken would surely come to pass. Robertson notes this beatitude is akin to the last in the Gospels, spoken to Thomas to discourage doubt; the witnesses agree faith is set forth as the ground of true happiness, since it receives God’s promises, and Mary’s ready believing of the incredible, contrasted with the aged priest’s doubt of a lesser thing, is honoured as the pattern of the trust that takes God at his word. Wesley notes Mary probably had in mind the unbelief of Zacharias; the witnesses agree the verse joins her blessedness to her faith, whether read ‘for there shall be’ or ‘that there shall be’ a performance of all the Lord had spoken to her.
46:
‘And Mary said, My soul doth magnify the Lord.’ The witnesses open the Magnificat, the first of Luke’s great canticles.

The fathers set its key. Ambrose: ‘as evil came into the world by a woman, so also is good introduced by women’; and as Mary was the greater, ‘she uttered the fuller prophecy.’ Basil: the Virgin ‘with lofty thoughts… contemplates the boundless mystery… magnifying God.’ To magnify is not to make God great but to ascribe greatness to him.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes notes ‘to magnify’ means to extol or celebrate (Psalms 34:3). Clarke and Robertson note the song is ‘a counterpart of the song of Hannah’ (1 Samuel 2:1-10), and that a few Latin manuscripts assign it to Elisabeth, though Mary is correct. Ellicott traces its place in Christian worship since Caesarius of Arles.

The Reformed Calvin notes the song’s three parts — thanksgiving for mercy to herself, celebration of God’s power and judgments, and the application to the promised redemption — and warns against the hypocrisy of singing God’s praises without a true heart. The shared, dominant note is that Mary, full of the Spirit, breaks into a song of praise that ascribes all greatness to God. The witnesses agree the Magnificat, modelled on Hannah’s song and steeped in the older Scriptures, is true prophecy and praise — the lowly handmaid magnifying the Lord for the great thing he had done in her and for his people. Adam Clarke notes the song is easily reduced to the hemistich form of Hebrew poetry, and the witnesses agree the Magnificat, modelled on Hannah’s song and steeped in the older Scriptures, is at once true prophecy and pure praise, the lowly handmaid ascribing all greatness to God for the great thing he had done in her and was about to do for his people. Ellicott traces the canticle’s place in Western worship since Caesarius of Arles; the witnesses agree the Magnificat, the first of Luke’s great songs, ascribes all greatness to God, a few Latin manuscripts assigning it to Elisabeth though Mary is the true singer.
47:
‘And my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Saviour.’ The witnesses note Mary’s exultation in God as her own Saviour.

The fathers draw out the joy. Basil: ‘the first-fruit of the Spirit is peace and joy,’ and the Virgin, having drunk in the Spirit’s graces, exults with ‘an unspeakable… bounding of the heart.’ Ambrose: ‘let every one have the spirit of Mary, so that he may rejoice in the Lord’; though one mother bore Christ after the flesh, ‘according to faith, Christ is the fruit of all.’

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Robertson notes the aorist ‘hath rejoiced’ (exult), Mary breathing ‘a spirit of composed rapture,’ and that ‘soul’ and ‘spirit’ are near synonyms in parallel. Barnes and Ellicott note God is called ‘Saviour’ as he saves from sin and death (Habakkuk 3:18).

The Wesleyan Wesley observes Mary ‘rejoiced in hope of salvation through faith in him… a blessing common to all true believers, more than in being his mother after the flesh.’ The shared, dominant note is that Mary’s joy is in God her Saviour — she counts herself among the redeemed who need salvation. The witnesses agree this is the mark of true praise: not pride in her unique honour, but rejoicing in the saving God; and, as Wesley and Ambrose press, her example is the pattern for every believer, whose deepest blessedness is not any outward privilege but to rejoice in the Saviour by faith. Theophylact notes that he magnifies God who worthily follows Christ; the witnesses agree Mary’s joy in ‘God my Saviour’ ranks her among the redeemed who need salvation, the mark of true praise being not pride in her unique honour but rejoicing in the saving God, an example, as Ambrose and Wesley press, for every believer whose deepest blessedness is to rejoice in the Saviour by faith. Robertson notes the aorist ‘rejoiced’ and that Mary breathes a spirit of composed rapture, soul and spirit being near synonyms; the witnesses agree God is called ‘Saviour’ (Habakkuk 3:18) as he saves from sin and death, Mary numbering herself among the redeemed.
48:
‘For he hath regarded the low estate of his handmaiden: for, behold… all generations shall call me blessed.’ The witnesses note God’s regard for Mary’s lowliness and her prophecy of lasting honour.

The fathers exalt her humility. Augustine: ‘O true lowliness, which has borne God to men… the lowliness of Mary was made the heavenly ladder by which God descended.’ The Greek Expositor: she ‘was content with things lowly, but now am I chosen to counsels unspeakable.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound ‘low estate’ (tapeinosis) as a ‘mean and despicable condition’ (so Calvin and Robertson) — the bride of a carpenter to be the mother of the Messiah — and stress that God ‘is not a respecter of persons’ (Romans 2:11, Psalms 138:6). Robertson and Clarke warn sharply that ‘all generations shall call me blessed’ is ‘a very different thing from the worship of Mary,’ the honour she sought being that of the blessed handmaid, not of a queen of heaven.

The shared, dominant note is that God looked in mercy on Mary’s humble condition, and that this regard, not any merit of hers, is the ground of her blessedness. The witnesses agree her foresight that all generations would call her blessed has been fulfilled, yet insist with one voice that to call her blessed is not to worship her: the verse magnifies the free grace of God toward the lowly, and Mary herself ascribes all the glory to him who ‘regarded’ his handmaid. Calvin insists tapeinosis denotes not modesty of mind but a mean and despised condition, so that Mary disclaims all merit and ascribes every occasion of boasting to God’s undeserved goodness; the witnesses agree her foreseen blessedness among all generations magnifies the free grace of God toward the lowly, and is no warrant for her worship, the honour she sought being that of the blessed handmaid. Augustine calls Mary’s lowliness ‘the heavenly ladder by which God descended upon earth’; the witnesses agree God’s regard for her humble condition, not any merit of hers, is the ground of her blessedness, and that to call her blessed is not to worship her.
49:
‘For he that is mighty hath done to me great things; and holy is his name.’ The witnesses note the mighty work and the holiness of God.

The fathers name the ‘great things.’ Augustine: ‘a creature, you gave birth to the Creator; a servant, you brought forth the Lord.’ Titus of Bostra: the great thing is that ‘I, still a virgin, conceive… overcoming nature,’ so that any who disbelieve must ‘throw back the miracle upon the power of the Worker.’ Bede ties ‘great things’ back to ‘my soul doth magnify the Lord.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes notes the ‘great things’ are distinguished mercies, and ‘holy is his name’ ascribes to God freedom from all sin (Psalms 111:9). Clarke renders the underlying word as ‘abundant goodness,’ agreeing with the mercy that follows. Basil and Bede note God’s holiness is his being ‘apart from the earth,’ far above every creature.

The Reformed Calvin makes this the second part of the song, where Mary turns from her own case to celebrate God’s power and holiness in general. The shared, dominant note is that the Mighty One had done great things for Mary — chiefly the virgin conception of the incarnate Lord — and that his name is holy. The witnesses agree the miracle is to be referred wholly to God’s power, not Mary’s virtue, and that the fitting response to such grace is to adore the holiness and might of the God who wrought it. Barnes notes ‘holy is his name’ ascribes to God freedom from sin and impurity, the name standing for God himself; the witnesses agree the great things done for Mary — chiefly the virgin conception of the incarnate Lord — are to be referred wholly to the power of the Mighty One, the fitting response being to adore the holiness and might of the God who wrought them. Titus of Bostra notes the great thing is the virgin conception overcoming nature, so that doubters must throw the miracle back upon the power of the Worker; the witnesses agree the response to such grace is to adore the holiness and might of God, whose name is holy, set apart above every creature.
50:
‘And his mercy is on them that fear him from generation to generation.’ The witnesses note the wide reach of God’s mercy to all who fear him.

The fathers mark the breadth. Bede notes Mary turns ‘from God’s special gifts to his general dealings’: not only for her has he done great things, but ‘in every nation he that fears God is accepted.’ The Greek Expositor and Origen note the mercy ‘extends to eternity from generation to generation.’

The grammatical-historical commentators define the fear. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain it is not the dread of a cruel master but the reverential, filial fear of a dutiful child (Acts 10:2); Ellicott notes the words ‘almost foreshadow the Gospel of the Apostle of the Gentiles,’ those who fear God being found in every nation (Acts 10:35).

The Reformed Gill notes that this fear, though not the cause of God’s mercy, describes those on whom it is shown — the vessels of mercy. The shared, dominant note is that God’s mercy, displayed so signally in Mary, flows perpetually to all who fear him, in every generation and nation. The witnesses agree the verse lifts the song from the particular to the universal: the same mercy that chose the lowly handmaid is the abiding portion of all who reverence God, a foretaste of the gospel’s reach to the Gentiles. Origen notes the mercy is not upon one generation but extends to eternity; the witnesses agree the verse lifts the song from the particular to the universal, the same mercy that chose the lowly handmaid being the abiding portion of all who fear God in every nation, a foretaste, as Ellicott observes, of the gospel’s reach to the Gentiles who fear God and work righteousness. Barnes distinguishes the filial fear of a dutiful child from the dread of a cruel master (Acts 10:2); the witnesses agree God’s mercy, displayed so signally in Mary, flows perpetually to all who fear him in every generation and nation. The Reformed Gill adds that this fear describes, though it does not cause, the vessels of mercy on whom God’s mercy is shown.
51:
‘He hath shewed strength with his arm; he hath scattered the proud.’ The witnesses note God’s mighty acts, putting down the proud.

The fathers read the ‘arm’ of Christ. Bede: ‘with his arm, i.e. with the very Son of God… the arm of God is said to be his word, by whom he made the world.’ Theophylact: ‘in his arm, that is, his incarnate Son, he has shown strength, seeing that nature was vanquished, a virgin bringing forth.’ The Greek Expositor reads the scattering of ‘every heart that was puffed up.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Clarke notes God’s ‘finger,’ ‘hand,’ and ‘arm’ mark ascending degrees of power (Exodus 15:16). Robertson notes the six aorists in the sentence and that ‘imagination’ means the proud’s intellectual insight. Ellicott marks the close parallel to Hannah (1 Samuel 2:3).

The Wesleyan Wesley notes Mary ‘speaks prophetically of those things as already done, which God was about to do by the Messiah.’ The shared, dominant note is that God shows his strength in scattering the proud — in the very humility of the Incarnation overturning human and spiritual pride. The witnesses agree the ‘arm’ is God’s own power, fittingly the incarnate Son, by whom the proud are confounded and the lowly exalted, the whole working a great reversal that the next verses unfold. Origen (in Bede) notes that to those who fear him God has done mighty things with his arm, the weak made strong; the witnesses agree the ‘arm’ is God’s own power, fittingly the incarnate Son, by whom in the very humility of the Incarnation human and spiritual pride is scattered and the lowly exalted, the whole working a great reversal that the following verses unfold. Clarke notes God’s finger, hand, and arm mark ascending degrees of power (Exodus 15:16); the witnesses agree the ‘arm’ is God’s own power, fittingly the incarnate Son, by whom the proud are scattered and the lowly raised, in close parallel to the song of Hannah. Robertson notes the six aorists in the sentence, neither corresponding to the English idiom, and that ‘imagination’ means the proud’s intellectual insight laid bare.
52:
‘He hath put down the mighty from their seats, and exalted them of low degree.’ The witnesses note the great reversal of God’s providence.

The fathers read it both temporally and spiritually. Bede: ‘through all generations of the world… the proud do not cease to fall, and the humble to be exalted.’ Cyril (quoted by Bede): ‘the mighty in knowledge were the evil spirits… the wise of the Gentiles, the Scribes and Pharisees… these he has put down,’ raising the humble.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Ellicott and Robertson note ‘the mighty’ (dynastas, whence ‘dynasty’) means rulers cast from their thrones (so 1 Timothy 6:15), and ‘low degree’ echoes Mary’s own ‘low estate’ (v.48). Barnes and Clarke see a reference to David raised over Saul.

The Reformed Calvin ascribes such changes ‘to the providence and judgment of God,’ that obscure persons may be elevated in the room of the fallen. The shared, dominant note is that God overturns the world’s order, casting down the proud and powerful and lifting up the humble. The witnesses see this fulfilled supremely in the gospel — the great brought low and the lowly exalted — and as the standing rule of God’s government, displayed already in Mary, the carpenter’s bride raised to be the mother of the Lord, and a warning to all human pride. Ellicott notes ‘of low degree’ is the adjective from which Mary’s own ‘low estate’ was formed; the witnesses agree God overturns the world’s order, casting down the proud and lifting up the humble, a rule fulfilled supremely in the gospel and displayed already in Mary, the carpenter’s bride raised to be the mother of the Lord — a standing warning to all human pride. Barnes and Clarke see a reference to David raised over Saul, Mary celebrating God’s mercies to her own ancestors; the witnesses agree God casts down the proud and powerful and lifts up the humble, a rule fulfilled supremely in the gospel and a warning to all human pride. Ellicott notes ‘the mighty’ (dynastas, whence ‘dynasty’) means rulers cast from their thrones, as in the ‘blessed and only Potentate’ of 1 Timothy 6:15.
53:
‘He hath filled the hungry with good things; and the rich he hath sent empty away.’ The witnesses note God’s feeding of the hungry and emptying of the self-sufficient.

The fathers read it spiritually. The Gloss notes the verse passes from casting down the mighty to ‘the impoverishing of the rich and the filling of the poor.’ Basil: ‘spiritually all mankind suffered hunger except the Jews,’ who had the oracles; yet not resting on the Incarnate Word they ‘were sent away empty.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes contrasts the poor who come for daily bread with the rich who come ‘with lofty hearts and insatiable desires’ and are sent empty. Ellicott notes the verse ‘anticipates the beatitudes’ of Luke 6:21, with both literal and spiritual fulfilments — the hungry who crave a higher blessedness are filled, the self-satisfied sent away. Clarke draws the picture of God feeding multitudes at his gates.

The shared, dominant note is that God satisfies the needy and empties the proud and self-sufficient. The witnesses agree the ‘hungry’ are chiefly those who feel their spiritual want and long for the Saviour, and the ‘rich’ those who, full of themselves, seek nothing from God; the one filled with good things, the other sent empty. The verse, they note, foreshadows the beatitudes and the whole gospel, which lifts up the poor in spirit and turns away those who trust in their own sufficiency. Adam Clarke pictures God as feeding multitudes at his gates, the poor coming through sense of want and the rich through lust of gain, sent empty; the witnesses agree the ‘hungry’ are chiefly those who feel their spiritual want and long for the Saviour, the ‘rich’ those full of themselves who seek nothing from God, the verse foreshadowing the beatitudes and the whole gospel. Ellicott notes the verse anticipates the beatitudes of Luke 6:21, with both literal and spiritual fulfilments; the witnesses agree the hungry who crave a higher blessedness are filled, while the self-satisfied, full of their own sufficiency, are sent empty away. Basil adds that all mankind hungered save the Jews, who had the oracles, yet not resting on the Incarnate Word were themselves sent empty away.
54:
‘He hath holpen his servant Israel, in remembrance of his mercy.’ The witnesses note God’s succouring of Israel in fulfilment of his promise.

The fathers read ‘Israel’ spiritually. The Gloss: he helped his servant by ‘becoming visible among men,’ making Israel (‘him who sees God’) his servant. Basil: not Israel after the flesh but ‘the spiritual Israel… straining their eyes to see God by faith.’ Theophylact allows Israel after the flesh too, since many of that body believed.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘hath holpen’ means to lay hold of with a view to succour, and ‘servant’ (paidos) here is ‘servant,’ not ‘son.’ Ellicott marks the word recalls the ‘servant of Jehovah’ of Isaiah 41:8. Clarke sees the reviving of the fallen house of David.

The Reformed Calvin notes the metaphor — God lays hold of Israel by the hand to lift him when ready to fall — for ‘the state of the nation was so fallen’ that its restoration was beyond ordinary hope. The shared, dominant note is that God came to the help of his people, remembering the mercy he had promised. The witnesses agree the ‘help’ is the redemption now dawning in Christ, raising up the fallen and fulfilling, to the true Israel of faith, the covenant of mercy made of old. Theophylact notes the grace of Christ extends even to the dead, since through him all shall rise, and that God fulfilled what he promised to Abraham; the witnesses agree the ‘help’ God gave Israel is the redemption now dawning in Christ, raising up the fallen and fulfilling, to the true Israel of faith, the covenant of mercy made of old. Robertson notes ‘servant’ (paidos) here means servant, not son, recalling the servant of Jehovah of Isaiah 41:8; the witnesses agree the ‘help’ God gave is the redemption now dawning in Christ, raising up the fallen nation in remembrance of his mercy. Theophylact allows the word may apply to Israel after the flesh too, since out of that body multitudes believed and became Abraham’s children by faith. Clarke sees in it the reviving of the fallen house of David, ready to fall and rise no more till Jesus restored the line.
55:
‘As he spake to our fathers, to Abraham, and to his seed for ever.’ The witnesses note the rooting of the redemption in the promise to Abraham.

The grammatical-historical commentators clarify the construction. Ellicott and Calvin note ‘Abraham and his seed’ belongs not in apposition to ‘fathers’ but to ‘remember his mercy… to Abraham and his seed for ever’ (v.54); the mercy shown to Abraham was ‘working even yet.’ Barnes ties it to the promise of Genesis 22:17-18.

The Reformed Gill notes the ‘fathers’ are David, Jacob, Isaac, and especially Abraham, to whom the seed was promised; the Wesleyan Wesley reads ‘his seed’ as ‘his spiritual seed: all true believers.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the redemption Mary celebrates is the keeping of the ancient promise sworn to Abraham and his seed for ever. The grammatical commentators clarify that the mercy ‘for ever’ reaches back to the patriarch and forward to all his children. The witnesses see in this the faithfulness of God: the great thing done in Mary is no novelty but the fulfilment of the covenant of grace made of old, in which salvation was promised to Abraham and, through his seed, to all the families of the earth — the spiritual seed, as Wesley adds, being all who believe. Ellicott clarifies that the mercy shown to Abraham was working even yet, the ‘for ever’ reaching back to the patriarch and forward to all his seed; the witnesses agree the great thing done in Mary is no novelty but the fulfilment of the covenant of grace, salvation promised to Abraham and, through his seed, to all the families of the earth — the spiritual seed, as Wesley adds, being all who believe. Gill notes the ‘fathers’ are David, Jacob, Isaac, and especially Abraham, to whom the seed was promised (Genesis 22:17-18); the witnesses agree the redemption is the keeping of an ancient promise, salvation reaching through Abraham’s seed to all who believe. Ellicott clarifies the construction: ‘Abraham and his seed’ belongs to ‘remember his mercy… for ever,’ not in apposition to ‘fathers.’
56:
‘And Mary abode with her about three months, and returned to her own house.’ The witnesses note the length of Mary’s stay and its purpose.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time. Lightfoot and Gill note ‘three months’ was the rabbinic span for ascertaining pregnancy, so Mary had ‘full satisfaction of the truth of the sign’ of Elisabeth’s conception (v.36). Ellicott and Clarke debate whether she stayed for John’s birth or departed just before.

The fathers note the benefit. Ambrose holds she abode so long ‘not only for the sake of friendship… but for the increase of so great a prophet’ — if at her coming the babe leaped, how much grace did her continued presence confer. Theophylact reckons the months that complete the nine of John’s gestation.

The Reformed Calvin notes the birth of John was ‘distinguished by various miracles’ that promised something great in the child, and that Mary stayed probably till the birth. The shared, dominant note is that Mary remained with Elisabeth about three months — long enough to confirm the angel’s sign and to share in the joy and holy converse of that household — and then returned home. The witnesses see in the stay both a confirmation of Mary’s faith, the promised sign proving true before her eyes, and, with Ambrose, a season of grace surrounding the forerunner’s birth. Lightfoot notes the three months were a recognised span among the doctors for knowing whether a woman was with child; the witnesses agree Mary’s stay both confirmed the angel’s sign before her eyes and, with Ambrose, made a season of grace around the forerunner’s birth, the Virgin sharing the holy joy of that household before returning to her own home at Nazareth. Ambrose holds Mary abode so long not only for friendship but for the increase of so great a prophet, her presence conferring grace; the witnesses agree the three months confirmed the angel’s sign and surrounded the forerunner’s birth with a season of holy joy before Mary returned home. Lightfoot notes the three months were a span well known among the doctors for ascertaining whether a woman was with child.
57:
‘Now Elisabeth’s full time came that she should be delivered; and she brought forth a son.’ The few voices note the birth of John in God’s appointed time.

The fathers mark the fullness. Ambrose observes that ‘the word signifying fullness is nowhere used except at the birth of the righteous’ — ‘the life of the righteous has fullness, but the days of the wicked are empty.’ Chrysostom holds the Lord delayed her delivery ‘that her joy might be increased, and her fame the greater.’

The grammatical-historical Clarke and the Puritan Henry note the natural course: though conceived by miracle, John ‘continued in the womb according to the ordinary course of nature’ (as did our Saviour), and ‘promised mercies are to be expected when the full time for them is come, and not before.’ Gill notes the nine months complete.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: at the appointed fullness of time Elisabeth bore a son. The fathers see in the word ‘full time’ a mark of the righteous, whose life is full; and Henry the lesson that God’s mercies come in their season. The witnesses agree the long-barren wife, now delivered of the forerunner, displays both the faithfulness of God to his promise and the increase of joy that follows his unhurried timing — the child of the promise born at the hour God had set. Gill notes the nine months of her going with child were now complete; the witnesses agree the long-barren wife, delivered of the forerunner at the appointed fullness of time, displays both the faithfulness of God to his promise and the increase of joy that follows his unhurried timing, the child of the promise born at the very hour God had set, his life ‘full’ as the fathers mark of the righteous. Chrysostom holds the Lord delayed her delivery that her joy might be increased and her fame the greater; the witnesses agree the long-barren wife, delivered at the appointed fullness of time, displays the faithfulness of God, the child of the promise born at the very hour he had set.
58:
‘Her neighbours and her cousins heard… and they rejoiced with her.’ The witnesses note the joy of kindred at God’s mercy to Elisabeth.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the words. Ellicott notes ‘cousins’ is better ‘kindred,’ and that ‘had shewed great mercy’ is literally ‘magnified his mercy,’ an echo of the Magnificat (v.46). Robertson notes the imperfect ‘rejoiced with her,’ picturing continual mutual joy.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the lesson that to rejoice with those whom God has favoured is ‘a duty of humanity, charity, and religion’; barrenness having been a reproach, the kindred now rejoiced that it was rolled away. Gill notes the neighbours and relations at Hebron.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the report of the birth spread, and Elisabeth’s kindred rejoiced with her at the great mercy God had shown. The grammatical commentators note the echo of the Magnificat — God had ‘magnified his mercy’ — and Clarke the duty of shared joy. The witnesses see in the rejoicing of neighbours and kin both the lifting of the reproach of barrenness and a fitting human echo of the divine mercy, the community gathering to glorify God for the child of the promise. Gill notes the neighbours and relations dwelt at Hebron and the parts about it; the witnesses agree the report of the birth spread and the kindred rejoiced at the great mercy God had shown, the lifting of the reproach of barrenness drawing forth a fitting human echo of the divine mercy, the community gathering to glorify God for the child of the promise. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘rejoiced with her’ pictures the continual mutual joy of the kindred; the witnesses agree the lifting of the reproach of barrenness drew forth a fitting human echo of the divine mercy, which God had ‘magnified’ in the very word of the Magnificat. Ellicott notes ‘cousins’ is better rendered ‘kindred,’ a wider range of relations than our cousinship. Clarke draws the lesson that to rejoice with those whom God has favoured is a duty of humanity, charity, and religion.
59:
‘On the eighth day they came to circumcise the child; and they called him Zacharias, after the name of his father.’ The witnesses note the circumcision and the dispute over the name.

The fathers read the rite typically. Chrysostom notes circumcision, given to Abraham as a sign, ‘ceased through Christ, and baptism came in its place,’ though John was first rightly circumcised. Bede reads the neighbours’ wish to name him after his father as the Jews preferring ‘the righteousness which is the Law’ over ‘the grace of faith’ signified by the name John (‘the grace of God’). Ambrose notes Elisabeth named him by prophecy, not from her husband.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the custom. Barnes and Ellicott note the name was commonly given at circumcision (Genesis 21:3-4), often the father’s; Robertson notes the conative imperfect, ‘they tried to call’ him Zacharias. Lightfoot describes the circumcision blessings. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on Antioch, tangential.

The shared, dominant note is that on the eighth day the child was circumcised, and the neighbours assumed he would bear his father’s name. The witnesses see in the rite the admission of John into the covenant, and, with the fathers, a turning-point — the last of the old order ushering in the grace of the new; and in the dispute over the name the providence that would, against custom, fix on him the God-given name John, the herald of grace. Lightfoot describes the blessings said at circumcision, when the child was entered into the covenant of Abraham and commonly received his name; the witnesses agree the rite admitted John into the covenant, and, with the fathers, marked a turning-point — the last of the old order ushering in the grace of the new — while the dispute over the name set the stage for the providence that would fix on him the God-given name John. Chrysostom notes circumcision, a sign given to Abraham, ceased through Christ, baptism coming in its place, though John was first rightly circumcised; the witnesses agree the rite admitted John into the covenant and marked the last of the old order ushering in the grace of the new.
60:
‘And his mother answered and said, Not so; but he shall be called John.’ The few voices note Elisabeth’s insistence on the God-given name.

The grammatical-historical Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott note this was the name the angel had appointed (v.13), of which Zacharias had probably informed Elisabeth by writing, since the tablet was in frequent use. Clarke draws out the meaning of the names — John, ‘the grace or mercy of Jehovah.’

The Reformed Calvin and the Wesleyan Wesley hold Elisabeth spoke ‘by revelation, or a particular impulse from God’; though Calvin allows Zacharias may have told her in writing the name the angel enjoined. Gill notes she answered as the child’s mother.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: against the expectation of the kindred, Elisabeth declared the child should be called John. The grammatical commentators trace this to the angel’s command, communicated by the dumb Zacharias in writing; some, with Wesley, see also a prophetic impulse. The witnesses agree the naming was no family choice but obedience to God: the forerunner of grace bears the name God gave, ‘the grace of Jehovah,’ and Elisabeth, whether by her husband’s word or the Spirit’s prompting, holds firmly to it against all custom. Clarke draws out the meaning of the names — John, ‘the grace or mercy of Jehovah’ — given by divine appointment; the witnesses agree the naming was no family choice but obedience to God, Elisabeth holding firmly to the name the angel had given, whether by her husband’s written word or a prophetic impulse of the Spirit, against all the custom of her kindred. Gill notes Elisabeth answered as the child’s mother, and Wesley that she spoke doubtless by revelation; the witnesses agree the naming was no family choice but obedience to God, the forerunner of grace bearing the name God gave, ‘the grace of Jehovah,’ against all the custom of the kindred. Clarke notes that the names of this family were remarkable, each pointing to some circumstance of God’s grace in conception or birth.
61:
‘And they said unto her, There is none of thy kindred that is called by this name.’ The few voices note the kindred’s objection from family custom.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the custom. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott note the Jewish tribes and families kept their genealogies distinct, giving children the names of their ancestors, partly from reverence and partly to avoid confusion in the genealogical tables. Gill notes that John was a common name in Israel, though not in Elisabeth’s family.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the kindred objected that no one of the family bore the name John, expecting a family name. The grammatical commentators trace the objection to the strong Jewish custom of naming children after ancestors, both out of respect and to keep the genealogies clear.

The shared, dominant note is the collision of human custom with divine appointment. The relations, reasoning from family tradition, could see no warrant for so unwonted a name; but the name had been given by God, not chosen by men. The witnesses see in the objection the natural resistance of custom to the new thing God was doing, and in the parents’ firmness the priority of God’s word over even hallowed human usage — the herald of the new covenant fittingly receiving a name that broke with the old family pattern, marking him as set apart for God’s own purpose. Ellicott notes the absence of the name from the family may show Zacharias was not of the Sadducean high-priestly circle, among whom it later appears; the witnesses agree the kindred’s objection sprang from the strong Jewish custom of naming children after ancestors, and that the parents’ firmness shows the priority of God’s word over even hallowed human usage, the herald of the new covenant fittingly breaking the old family pattern. Gill notes that John, though a common name in Israel, was not found in Elisabeth’s family; the witnesses agree the kindred’s objection from custom collided with the divine appointment, the parents’ firmness showing the priority of God’s word over even hallowed human usage.
62:
‘And they made signs to his father, how he would have him called.’ The few voices note the signs made to the dumb (and deaf) Zacharias.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw an inference. Clarke and Ellicott hold that their making signs ‘almost certainly’ shows Zacharias was deaf as well as dumb, passed into the condition of a deaf-mute; otherwise they could simply have asked him. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘made signs’ (repeated action) and the indirect question, ‘what he would wish him called.’ Gill cites the rabbinic notice that a deaf-mute communicates by nods and signs. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: unable to speak with Zacharias, the kindred made signs to learn his wish for the name. The grammatical commentators infer from this that his affliction included deafness, not speechlessness only, since signs rather than speech were used.

The shared, dominant note is that the company turned from the mother to the father for a decision, communicating by gesture because he could neither hear nor speak. The witnesses see in the detail both a confirmation of the completeness of Zacharias’s chastisement — the doubting priest left for a season both deaf and dumb — and the providence ordering that the father, by his written word, should presently confirm the very name his wife had declared, so that the divine appointment of ‘John’ might be doubly attested. Robertson notes the imperfect ‘made signs’ pictures repeated gesturing, and the indirect question what the father would wish; the witnesses agree the turning from the mother to the father, communicating by gesture, confirms the completeness of Zacharias’s chastisement — both deaf and dumb — and prepares the way for his written word to confirm, doubly, the divine appointment of the name John. Gill cites the rabbinic notice that a deaf-mute communicates by nods and signs; the witnesses infer from the signs that Zacharias was deaf as well as dumb, his chastisement complete, and the company turned to the father whose written word would presently confirm the name his wife had declared.
63:
‘And he asked for a writing table, and wrote, saying, His name is John. And they marvelled all.’ The witnesses note the written word confirming the name.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the tablet. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson note the ‘writing table’ (pinakidion) was a small wooden board coated with wax, written on with a stylus, sometimes a physician’s note-book. Ellicott marks the emphatic present, ‘His name is John’ — ‘not a question to be discussed… the name had been given already’ — and that the general marvel confirms Zacharias’s previous deafness.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Zacharias, asked by signs, called for a tablet and wrote, ‘His name is John,’ and all marvelled. The grammatical commentators note the emphatic present tense — the name was not open to debate but already divinely fixed — and the wonder of the company.

The shared, dominant note is the full agreement of father and mother, by separate means, on the God-given name. Zacharias, who could not speak, wrote the very name Elisabeth had declared, and the unexpected concord, against all family custom, struck the onlookers with amazement. The witnesses see in the written ‘His name is John’ the obedience of faith — the once-doubting priest now submitting wholly to the angel’s word — and the providence that made the naming a public sign, the marvel preparing the way for the still greater wonder of the next verse, his loosened tongue. Clarke notes such tablets, smeared with whiting or wax, were used by the children of the East to write upon; the witnesses agree Zacharias’s written ‘His name is John’ was the obedience of faith, the once-doubting priest submitting wholly to the angel’s word, the unexpected concord of father and mother against all custom striking the company with amazement and preparing for the greater wonder of his loosed tongue. Ellicott marks the emphatic present, ‘His name is John’ — not a question to be discussed, the name already given; the witnesses agree the unexpected concord of father and mother, against all custom, struck the company with amazement and prepared for the greater wonder of his loosed tongue.
64:
‘And his mouth was opened immediately… and he spake, and praised God.’ The witnesses note the loosing of Zacharias’s tongue and his praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the cure. Robertson notes ‘immediately’ (a favourite Lukan word) and the zeugma — ‘opened’ suits the mouth, with ‘loosed’ understood for the tongue. Barnes and Clarke note that for nine months he had been dumb, his speech now restored the instant the child was named, so that ‘in his nine months’ silence he had learned the proper use of’ speech, and used it first to praise God.

The Reformed Calvin notes God ‘put honour on the birth of his prophet by restoring speech to his father,’ the benefit delayed till that day to fix men’s eyes on John; and Zacharias praised God, uniting ‘with his own dishonour the praises of the divine glory.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: at the naming of the child, Zacharias’s mouth was opened and he spoke, praising God. The grammatical commentators note the instant cure, exactly as the angel had foretold (v.20), and that his first words were thanksgiving. The witnesses see in this both the fulfilment of the angel’s word — the silence ending when obedience was complete — and the fruit of his chastening: the once-unbelieving priest, restored, turns his recovered tongue at once to the praise of God, as the Benedictus will unfold. Barnes notes that for one act of disbelief the calamity had come, and now with true gratitude he offered praise; the witnesses agree the instant cure, exactly as the angel had foretold, both fulfilled the divine word and displayed the fruit of his chastening, the restored priest turning his recovered tongue at once to the praise of God, as the Benedictus will unfold. Calvin notes God put honour on the prophet’s birth by restoring speech to his father, the benefit delayed till that day to fix men’s eyes on John; the witnesses agree the instant cure fulfilled the angel’s word (v.20) and the restored priest turned his recovered tongue at once to praise.
65:
‘And fear came on all that dwelt round about them.’ The witnesses note the reverent awe that spread through the hill country.

The fathers read its purpose. Theophylact: as the people marvelled at Zacharias’s silence, so now at his speech, ‘that from these two circumstances all might believe there was something great in the child’; for ‘fore-running signs prepare the way for the forerunner of the truth.’ Bede joins the same thought.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the fear. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson explain it is not terror but ‘religious reverence’ or awe at contact with the supernatural (Acts 9:31); the events ‘were noised abroad’ (imperfect) through all the hill country of Judaea. Ellicott notes the spread from the neighbourhood to the wider district.

The Reformed Calvin notes the fear ‘proceeded from a feeling of the divine power,’ for God’s works ought to be contemplated ‘with such reverence as to affect our minds with seriousness.’ The shared, dominant note is that the remarkable events — the silence, the naming, the loosed tongue — filled the neighbours with religious awe and were talked of through the region. The witnesses agree this awe was the fitting response to God’s evident working, and, as the fathers note, served the purpose of marking out the child as one set apart for some great office, preparing men to heed the forerunner when he should appear. Bede joins Theophylact in noting that fore-running signs prepare the way for the forerunner of the truth; the witnesses agree the remarkable events — the silence, the naming, the loosed tongue — filled the neighbours with religious awe and were talked of through the hill country, the awe being the fitting response to God’s evident working and a means of marking out the child for his great office. Calvin notes the fear proceeded from a feeling of the divine power, which God’s works ought to stir in us; the witnesses agree the remarkable events filled the neighbours with religious awe and were noised abroad through the hill country, marking out the child for his great office. Theophylact notes that as the people marvelled at Zacharias’s silence, so now at his speech, that from both they might believe something great in the child.
66:
‘What manner of child shall this be! And the hand of the Lord was with him.’ The witnesses note the people’s wondering question and Luke’s comment on God’s guiding hand.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘What then’ (not ‘who’) marks their prediction of a marvellous career; they ‘laid them up’ in their hearts as Mary did (Luke 2:19). Barnes and Ellicott explain ‘the hand of the Lord’ as the guidance, protection, and favour of God (Acts 11:21), a Hebrew idiom; some read ‘for the hand of the Lord was with him’ as the reason for their question.

The Wesleyan Wesley glosses ‘the hand of the Lord’ as ‘the peculiar power and blessing of God.’ The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on the sevenfold Spirit, loosely related. The shared, dominant note is that all who heard pondered the remarkable events and asked what this child would become, while Luke adds that God’s hand was upon him.

The witnesses agree the people, laying up these wonders in their hearts, rightly foresaw a great destiny for the child; and that Luke’s own note — ‘the hand of the Lord was with him’ — explains the supernatural happenings, marking John as guided and blessed of God from his infancy. They see in the wondering question the awakening expectation of the people, and in the divine hand the providence that would shape the forerunner for his work, the child set apart and kept by God for the day of his showing to Israel. Robertson notes they ‘laid up’ these things in their hearts, as Mary did, pondering what the child would become; the witnesses agree the people rightly foresaw a great destiny, and that Luke’s own note — ‘the hand of the Lord was with him’ — explains the supernatural happenings, marking John as guided and blessed of God from infancy and kept for the day of his showing to Israel. Barnes and Ellicott explain ‘the hand of the Lord’ as the guidance, protection, and favour of God (Acts 11:21), a Hebrew idiom; the witnesses agree the people rightly foresaw a great destiny, while Luke’s note marks John as guided and blessed of God from infancy.
67:
‘And his father Zacharias was filled with the Holy Ghost, and prophesied.’ The witnesses note the inspired song of the restored priest, the Benedictus.

The fathers mark the grace. Ambrose: God ‘not only restores what he has taken away, but grants us favors even beyond our expectations’ — ‘he that was long silent prophesies’; let none despair, for ‘God knows how to change his sentence,’ Chrysostom adding he received the Spirit’s grace ‘fully.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound ‘prophesied.’ Barnes and Ellicott note it here means an inspired utterance of praise (1 Corinthians 14:24-25) as well as foretelling; Robertson notes nearly every phrase is from the Psalms and Prophets, Zacharias having ‘caught the Messianic message in its highest meaning.’ Clarke takes ‘prophesy’ in its proper sense of foretelling future events.

The Puritan Henry notes God not only forgave Zacharias his unbelief but ‘filled him with the Holy Ghost’ and made his once-silenced mouth the vehicle of prophecy. The shared, dominant note is that Zacharias, his speech restored, was filled with the Spirit and broke into prophetic praise. The witnesses agree the Benedictus is true prophecy, steeped in the older Scriptures, speaking chiefly of Christ and only incidentally of John; and they see in the once-doubting priest now prophesying a signal proof of the grace that turns chastisement into blessing. Robertson notes the Benedictus (verses 68-79) is likely the ‘blessing God’ mentioned at verse 64, nearly every phrase drawn from the Psalms and Prophets; the witnesses agree the once-doubting priest, his speech restored and filled with the Spirit, now prophesies chiefly of Christ and only incidentally of John, a signal proof of the grace that turns chastisement into blessing and makes a silenced mouth the vehicle of prophecy. Ambrose draws the lesson that God restores beyond expectation, the long-silent now prophesying, so that none from consciousness of past sin should despair; the witnesses agree the Benedictus is true prophecy, steeped in Psalms and Prophets, speaking chiefly of Christ and only incidentally of John. Chrysostom adds that Zacharias received the grace of the Holy Spirit not in any measure but fully, and the gift of prophecy with it.
68:
‘Blessed be the Lord God of Israel; for he hath visited and redeemed his people.’ The witnesses note the opening blessing of the Benedictus for God’s redemption.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes and Robertson note ‘hath visited’ (epeskepsato) means to look upon in order to help (Matthew 25:43), the four silent centuries since the prophets now ended as God ‘looked on’ Israel again; ‘redemption’ (lytrosin) originally political but with a moral and spiritual basis (verses 75, 77). Ellicott traces the hymn’s place in Western worship since Caesarius of Arles.

The Wesleyan Clarke notes Zacharias praises God for two benefits — he has ‘visited’ and ‘ransomed’ his people — speaking prophetically of things determined as though already done; God visits his people ‘in the incarnation of Jesus Christ.’

The Reformed Calvin notes Zacharias begins with thanksgiving, describing ‘the fulfillment of the redemption formerly promised in Christ, on which the safety… of the church depended,’ and calls God ‘the Lord God of Israel’ because the Redeemer was promised to Abraham’s seed. The shared, dominant note is that Zacharias blesses God for visiting and redeeming his people in the coming Christ. The witnesses agree the visitation is the Incarnation, the long-awaited breaking of God’s silence; and the redemption, though couched in terms of national deliverance, is at heart the spiritual ransom the Messiah brings. Clarke notes Zacharias gives God the highest title, ‘Jehovah the God of Israel,’ for it is God himself who visits his people in the incarnation of Christ; the witnesses agree the visitation is the Incarnation, the long-awaited breaking of God’s four-century silence, and the redemption, though couched in terms of national deliverance, is at heart the spiritual ransom the Messiah brings to his people. Robertson notes ‘hath visited’ means to look upon in order to help (Matthew 25:43), and ‘redemption’ though once political has a spiritual basis; the witnesses agree the visitation is the Incarnation, the breaking of God’s long silence, the redemption the spiritual ransom the Messiah brings. Calvin notes Zacharias begins with thanksgiving, describing the fulfilment of the redemption formerly promised in Christ, on which the safety of the church depended.
69:
