‘And hath raised up an horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David.’ The witnesses note the ‘horn of salvation,’ the mighty Redeemer of David’s line.

The fathers read the figure. Theophylact: God, who seemed asleep, ‘has risen up and trodden down the evil spirits.’ Chrysostom: ‘by a horn he means power, glory, and honor,’ from the horned beasts. Bede: the kingdom of Christ is ‘the horn of salvation… as reaching beyond the world and the delights of the flesh,’ David and Solomon being consecrated with the horn of oil.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson note the horn is a symbol of strength (the rhinoceros, the bull) and so of a mighty Saviour able to save (Psalms 132:17), ‘in the house of David’ because Christ came of David’s line. Ellicott traces the symbol to the same Psalm.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as ‘saving power’: when David’s throne was cast down and the people scattered, the hope of salvation seemed lost, yet God made the horn to bud (Psalms 132:17). The shared, dominant note is that God has raised up in David’s house a mighty Saviour — the ‘horn of salvation.’ The witnesses agree the figure speaks of strength and royal power, the Messiah of David’s line raised up to save his people with a salvation that reaches beyond the world; the budding horn the revival of hope when all seemed lost. Adam Clarke notes the horn is the well-known emblem of strength, glory, and power in sacred and profane writers alike, the strength of horned beasts lying in their horns; the witnesses agree the figure speaks of a mighty and royal Saviour raised up in David’s line to save his people with a salvation reaching beyond the world, the budding horn the revival of hope when David’s throne seemed cast down for ever. Bede notes Christ’s kingdom is the horn of salvation as reaching beyond the world and the delights of the flesh, David and Solomon being consecrated with the horn of oil; the witnesses agree the figure speaks of a mighty and royal Saviour raised up in David’s line.
70:
‘As he spake by the mouth of his holy prophets, which have been since the world began.’ The witnesses note that the redemption fulfils the witness of all the prophets.

The fathers mark the long testimony. Theophylact cites Micah on Bethlehem (Micah 5:2), noting ‘all the prophets spoke of the Incarnation.’ The Greek Expositor: God ‘spoke through them, and their speech was not of man.’ Bede: ‘all the Scriptures of the Old Testament were a constant prophecy of Christ,’ from Adam onward.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes notes all the prophets — Jacob (Genesis 49:10), Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15), Isaiah — foretold the Messiah, ‘since the world began’ being a general expression; Robertson renders it ‘from of old.’ Ellicott points to the first gospel of Genesis 3:15.

The Reformed Calvin notes Zacharias adduces ‘all the prophets’ as witnesses, that the salvation brought by Christ ‘may not be thought doubtful on the score of novelty.’ The shared, dominant note is that the redemption now come was foretold by God through his prophets from the earliest times. The witnesses agree this both vindicates the gospel against the charge of novelty and displays the faithfulness of God: the Christ raised up in David’s house is the very Redeemer whom Law and Prophets, from Eden onward, with one voice foretold. Ellicott points to the first gospel of Genesis 3:15 and the promises to Abraham as among the earliest of these prophecies; the witnesses agree the redemption now come was foretold by God through his prophets from the earliest times, which both vindicates the gospel against the charge of novelty and displays God’s faithfulness, the Christ of David’s house being the very Redeemer whom Law and Prophets foretold. Calvin notes Zacharias adduces all the prophets as witnesses, that the salvation may not be thought doubtful for its novelty; the witnesses agree the redemption now come was foretold from the earliest times, the Christ of David’s house being the very Redeemer whom Law and Prophets foretold from Eden onward. Bede notes that all the Scriptures of the Old Testament were a constant prophecy of Christ, from our father Adam onward.
71:
‘That we should be saved from our enemies, and from the hand of all that hate us.’ The witnesses note the salvation from enemies, understood chiefly of spiritual foes.

The fathers spiritualise the enemies. Origen (in Bede): ‘let us not suppose this refers to our bodily enemies, but our ghostly’; the Lord came ‘mighty in battle to destroy all our enemies.’ Bede joins this exposition of the ‘horn of salvation.’

The grammatical-historical commentators note both senses. Clarke insists that, since Zacharias spoke by the Spirit, the salvation ‘must necessarily be understood in a spiritual sense’ — Satan, death, and sin the enemies. Ellicott allows the ‘enemies’ present to Zacharias may have included the Roman and Herodian powers. Barnes lists sin, the carnal nature, and Satan (Genesis 3:15).

The Reformed Calvin holds the chief war of the church is ‘not with flesh and blood, but with Satan,’ though Christ delivers also from outward foes. The shared, dominant note is that the salvation Christ brings is deliverance from our enemies — and, the witnesses agree, chiefly the spiritual enemies of sin, Satan, and death. While Zacharias, in the language of prophecy, may have had in mind also the oppressors of Israel, the deeper and abiding sense, as Clarke and Calvin press, is the rescue of God’s people from the powers of darkness, that they might serve him without fear. Barnes lists the enemies as sin, the carnal nature, Satan and his angels, from whom the Messiah came to save (compare Genesis 3:15); the witnesses agree that, while Zacharias in the language of prophecy may have included the oppressors of Israel, the deeper and abiding sense is the rescue of God’s people from the powers of darkness, that they might serve him without fear. Calvin holds the chief war of the church is not with flesh and blood but with Satan, though Christ delivers also from outward foes; the witnesses agree the deeper sense of salvation from our enemies is the rescue of God’s people from sin, Satan, and death, that they might serve him without fear. Origen (in Bede) bids us not suppose this refers to bodily enemies, but to our ghostly foes, whom the Lord came mighty in battle to destroy.
72:
‘To perform the mercy promised to our fathers, and to remember his holy covenant.’ The witnesses note that the redemption keeps the covenant of mercy made with the fathers.

The fathers read the covenant. Bede: the Lord, born of David’s house, ‘will deliver us’ to fulfil the covenant with Abraham, to whom ‘was promised the gathering of the Gentiles.’ Theophylact: ‘he performed his mercy also to our forefathers in fulfilling all their hopes,’ multiplying Abraham’s seed in all nations by adoption. Basil notes God’s ‘word is in very truth… in place of an oath.’

The grammatical-historical Ellicott finds in the verse an echo of the very names — ‘performing mercy’ (John = ‘the Lord be merciful’), ‘remembering’ (Zacharias = ‘whom Jehovah remembers’), the ‘oath’ (Elisabeth = ‘the oath of my God’), a play obvious in the Hebrew. Barnes notes mercy to the children is kindness to the fathers.

The Reformed Calvin points to ‘the gracious mercy of God’ as the fountain of redemption, God being ‘mindful of his promise.’ The shared, dominant note is that the salvation now come fulfils the mercy God promised the fathers and keeps his holy covenant. The witnesses agree the redemption is no afterthought but the remembering of an ancient covenant of grace; and Ellicott’s observation, that the very names of the family are woven into the song, marks how fittingly the birth of John proclaims that ‘the Lord is merciful’ and ‘remembers’ his oath. Bede notes that to the patriarchs was promised the gathering of the Gentiles and the incarnation of Christ; the witnesses agree the salvation now come fulfils the mercy promised the fathers and keeps God’s holy covenant, no afterthought but the remembering of an ancient covenant of grace — and, as Ellicott observes, the very names of the family are woven into the song, proclaiming that the Lord is merciful and remembers his oath. Barnes notes mercy to the children is reckoned as kindness to the fathers, so the blessing of John’s birth was a remembering of the covenant; the witnesses agree the redemption is no afterthought but the keeping of an ancient covenant of grace, the very names of the family woven into the song.
73:
‘The oath which he sware to our father Abraham.’ The few voices note the oath underlying the covenant of redemption.

The grammatical-historical commentators anchor it. Barnes and Robertson point to the oath of Genesis 22:16-18, in which God swore by himself (because he could swear by no greater, Hebrews 6:13-14) to bless Abraham and his seed, now fulfilled in the Messiah; Robertson notes the oppression of the Gentiles in Zacharias’s mind. Ellicott marks the oath in apposition to the ‘covenant.’

The Reformed Calvin notes the oath is mentioned ‘to express more fully the firmness and sacredness of his truth,’ God condescending to ‘employ his name for the support of our weakness.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the covenant of mercy rests on the oath God sware to Abraham. The grammatical commentators trace it to Genesis 22:16-18, the oath confirmed by God swearing by himself; and Calvin draws the comfort that God added an oath to his promise as a gracious help to our weak faith. The witnesses see in the oath the unshakeable ground of the redemption: not a bare promise only, but a covenant sworn and sealed, certain to be performed, so that the salvation come in Christ stands on the very faithfulness and majesty of God. Ellicott marks the oath in apposition to the covenant, the firm ground on which the whole hope rests; the witnesses agree the covenant of mercy rests on the oath God sware to Abraham, confirmed by his swearing by himself, so that the salvation come in Christ stands not on a bare promise only but on a covenant sworn and sealed, certain to be performed by the faithfulness and majesty of God. Calvin draws the comfort that God added an oath to his promise as a gracious help to our weak faith, employing his own name; the witnesses agree the covenant of mercy rests on the oath sworn to Abraham (Genesis 22:16-18), so the salvation in Christ stands on the faithfulness of God. Robertson notes the oppression of the Gentiles seems to be in the mind of Zacharias, though he may not have grasped the spiritual Israel as Paul saw it.
74:
‘That he would grant unto us, that we… might serve him without fear.’ The witnesses note the end of redemption: fearless service of God.

The fathers mark the double gift. Chrysostom notes the union of two things ‘not easily found united’ — deliverance from danger and a glorious, fearless life — for many escape danger but fail of glory, and others win glory only through danger; but Christ gives both.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Ellicott and Barnes note this was ‘the end to which the covenant and the oath had all along been pointing’ — the service of worship and obedience, ‘without fear’ of enemies or death; Robertson notes ‘serve’ (latreuein) is the service rendered to God. Clarke draws out the deliverance unto holiness.

The Reformed Calvin presses the purpose: the redeemed are to ‘dedicate and consecrate themselves entirely to the Author of their salvation,’ for the final cause of redemption is ‘that, by a godly and holy life, men may glorify his name’ (1 Thessalonians 4:7). The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced. The shared, dominant note is that God delivers his people not for their ease only but that they may serve him without fear. The witnesses agree deliverance is the means and holy, grateful service the end — the redeemed freed from their enemies precisely that they might worship and obey God all their days, without the bondage of fear. Adam Clarke draws out the deliverance unto holiness — freed from our enemies that we may worship God in the spirit; the witnesses agree God delivers his people not for their ease only but that they may serve him without fear, deliverance the means and holy, grateful service the end, the redeemed freed from their enemies precisely that they might worship and obey God all their days. Calvin presses that the redeemed are to consecrate themselves wholly to the Author of their salvation, the final cause of redemption being a godly life that glorifies God (1 Thessalonians 4:7); the witnesses agree deliverance is the means and fearless service of God the end.
75:
‘In holiness and righteousness before him, all the days of our life.’ The witnesses note the character and constancy of the service redemption secures.

The fathers distinguish the terms. Chrysostom: ‘holiness is a proper observance of our duty towards God, righteousness of our duty towards man,’ and ‘before God,’ not before men as hypocrites, since ‘their praise is not of men but of God.’

The grammatical-historical commentators agree. Robertson and Ellicott note ‘holiness’ (hosiotes) is the Godward and ‘righteousness’ (dikaiosyne) the manward aspect of conduct (Ephesians 4:24); Barnes that it is ‘before him,’ spiritual and inward, ‘all the days’ to death.

The Reformed Calvin notes the two tables of the law — holiness answering to the first, righteousness to the second — so that ‘we serve God in a proper manner when our life has been framed to’ both. The shared, dominant note is that the service of the redeemed is to be marked by holiness toward God and righteousness toward men, sincere before God, and lasting all our days. The witnesses agree the verse defines the fearless service of the previous one: not a fitful or outward religion, but a whole-life devotion embracing both tables of the law, rendered in God’s sight and continued to the end — the proper fruit and aim of the redemption Christ brings. Adam Clarke and Calvin note holiness and righteousness answer to the two tables of the law, our duty toward God and toward men; the witnesses agree the service of the redeemed is to be marked by holiness toward God and righteousness toward men, sincere before God and lasting all our days — not a fitful or outward religion, but a whole-life devotion that is the proper fruit and aim of the redemption Christ brings. Chrysostom notes the service is ‘before God,’ not before men as hypocrites, since the believer’s praise is of God; the witnesses agree the service of the redeemed is to be marked by holiness toward God and righteousness toward men, sincere and lasting all the days of life. Robertson notes ‘holiness’ (hosiotes) is the Godward and ‘righteousness’ (dikaiosyne) the manward aspect of conduct (Ephesians 4:24).
76:
‘And thou, child, shalt be called the prophet of the Highest: for thou shalt go before the face of the Lord.’ The witnesses note Zacharias turning to address his infant son and his office.

The fathers mark the turn. Ambrose: ‘in prophesying of the Lord he rightly addresses the prophet,’ not silent about his own son’s gift; Origen suggests he hastened to speak to John, knowing the child would soon ‘be a sojourner in the wilderness.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the office. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson note John is ‘prophet of the Highest’ and forerunner, ‘going before the face of the Lord’ — an echo of Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 — the ‘Lord’ being Jehovah (the Messiah). Clarke draws out John’s dignity as prophet in both senses, foreteller and teacher.

The Reformed Calvin notes Zacharias speaks of the infant’s calling ‘as a thing already known,’ turning his eyes to God’s purpose. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on Jeroboam’s withered hand, tangential. The shared, dominant note is that Zacharias foretells his son’s office: the prophet of the Most High who goes before the Lord to prepare his way. The witnesses agree the song now turns briefly from Christ to his herald, yet even here points to Christ — John is great only as the forerunner of the greater, going before the face of the Lord whose coming he announces. Ambrose notes that prophecy itself is a gift of the Lord, so Zacharias rightly addresses the prophet his son; the witnesses agree the song now turns briefly from Christ to his herald, yet even here points to Christ, for John is great only as the forerunner of the greater, the prophet of the Most High going before the face of the Lord to prepare his way, an echo of Malachi and Isaiah. Origen suggests Zacharias hastened to speak to his son, knowing John would soon dwell in the wilderness; the witnesses agree the song turns briefly from Christ to his herald, yet points still to Christ, John being great only as the forerunner going before the face of the Lord (Malachi 3:1).
77:
‘To give knowledge of salvation unto his people by the remission of their sins.’ The witnesses note the substance of John’s preparatory work.

The fathers read it of Christ. Theophylact: ‘the Lord Jesus is salvation, but the knowledge of salvation… was given in John, who bore witness of Christ.’ Bede: lest men think a temporal salvation was meant, he adds ‘for the forgiveness of sins,’ for in no other way was Christ known to be God but as forgiving sins.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Ellicott notes this was ‘the object of the Baptist’s mission’: men, ‘wrapt in dreams of deliverance from outward enemies,’ needed to learn that salvation lay in ‘forgiveness for the sins of the past, and power to overcome sins in the future.’ Barnes notes salvation must be connected with the pardon of sin; Robertson, that this is ‘the aim and end of the work of the Forerunner.’

The Reformed Calvin touches ‘the principal subject of the gospel’: salvation consists in the remission of sins, since we are by nature condemned, and there is no escape but by God ‘not imputing our trespasses’ (2 Corinthians 5:19). The shared, dominant note is that John’s task was to give his people the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of sins. The witnesses agree this defines true salvation against every merely political hope: it is the pardon of sin, made known by the forerunner and accomplished by the Christ he heralds. Barnes notes that salvation must be connected with the pardon of sin, for God cannot treat men as his friends unless they come by repentance; the witnesses agree John’s task was to give his people the knowledge of salvation through forgiveness, defining true salvation against every merely political hope — the pardon of sin, made known by the forerunner and accomplished by the Christ he heralds. Theophylact notes the Lord Jesus is salvation, but the knowledge of salvation was given in John who bore witness of him; the witnesses agree John’s task defined true salvation against every political hope — the remission of sins, made known by the forerunner and accomplished by the Christ he heralds.
78:
‘Through the tender mercy of our God; whereby the dayspring from on high hath visited us.’ The witnesses note the source of redemption in God’s mercy and the figure of the dayspring.

The fathers read the dayspring as Christ. Theophylact: forgiveness comes ‘not for our works’ sake, but through his mercy’; Chrysostom: ‘the day-spring from on high (that is, Christ) has visited us, taking upon him our flesh.’ The Greek Expositor marvels at him ‘abiding on high yet present upon the earth.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the words. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson note ‘tender mercy’ is literally ‘bowels of mercy’ (the heart of God), and ‘dayspring’ (anatole) the rising of the sun or a star from on high (Isaiah 60:19). Lightfoot weighs whether ‘branch’ (Jeremiah 23:5) is meant, but the giving of light favours the dawn.

The Reformed Calvin extols the mercy that ‘made the day to dawn,’ proceeding from the very ‘bowels of mercy’ of God. The shared, dominant note is that the whole redemption springs from God’s tender mercy, and that Christ is the dayspring — the rising Sun from heaven — who has visited a world in darkness. The witnesses divide only on whether ‘branch’ or ‘dawn’ is the precise figure, but agree the sense: the merciful God has caused the light of his salvation to break, in Christ, upon those who sat in darkness. Ellicott notes ‘tender mercy’ is, after the Hebrew manner, the ‘bowels’ or heart of God, the pity that moved him to redeem; the witnesses agree the whole redemption springs from God’s tender mercy, and that Christ is the dayspring — the rising Sun from heaven — visiting a world in darkness, dividing only on whether ‘dawn’ or ‘Branch’ is the precise figure of the word anatole. Chrysostom reads the dayspring from on high as Christ visiting us by taking our flesh; the witnesses agree the whole redemption springs from God’s tender mercy, the rising Sun from heaven breaking upon a world in darkness, whether the figure be the dawn or the Branch of Jeremiah 23:5.
79:
‘To give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the way of peace.’ The witnesses note the purpose of the dayspring — light and peace to a benighted world.

The fathers expound the darkness. Bede: Christ is rightly the Day-spring, ‘disclosing the rising of the true light’; Chrysostom: the darkness is ‘error… and ungodliness’; Basil: ‘in thick darkness were the Gentile people sitting… until the rising light dispersed the darkness.’

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the figure. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson root it in Isaiah 9:2 (compare Matthew 4:16): benighted travellers, sitting in a dark ravine, wait for the morning to guide their feet, and the rising Sun lights them into ‘the way of peace’ (Psalms 107:10) — ‘peace’ including every blessedness.

The Reformed Calvin notes that ‘out of Christ there is no life-giving light in the world, but everything is covered by the… darkness of death.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ the dayspring shines on those sitting in darkness and the shadow of death, to guide them into peace. The witnesses agree the figure is of benighted travellers given light at last: the world, sunk in ignorance and ungodliness and under the shadow of death, is illumined by the rising Sun of righteousness, who guides the feet of his people into the way of peace with God. Robertson notes the shadow of death is combined with darkness in Psalms 107:10, the figure of prisoners and benighted travellers; the witnesses agree Christ the dayspring shines on those sitting in darkness and the shadow of death to guide them into peace, the world sunk in ignorance and ungodliness illumined by the rising Sun of righteousness, who guides the feet of his people into the way of peace with God. Calvin notes that out of Christ there is no life-giving light in the world, all being covered by the darkness of death; the witnesses agree Christ the dayspring shines on those in darkness and the shadow of death (Isaiah 9:2) to guide their feet into the way of peace with God.
80:
‘And the child grew, and waxed strong in spirit, and was in the deserts till the day of his shewing unto Israel.’ The witnesses note John’s growth, his desert preparation, and his hidden years.

The fathers mark the wilderness. Bede: ‘the future preacher of repentance… passes the first part of his life in the deserts,’ that he might the more boldly reclaim his hearers from the world. Theophylact: he ‘waxed strong in spirit, for together with his body… his spiritual gift increased.’ Origen: the Spirit was ‘ever growing in him.’

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes the imperfects ‘was growing’ and ‘waxed strong,’ the child increasing in strength of body and spirit; Barnes and Clarke that he dwelt in the hill country and deserts in obscurity ‘till the day of his showing’ — his public ministry at about thirty (Luke 3:23). Lightfoot weighs whether John was a true hermit, apart from synagogue and feasts.

The shared, dominant note is that John grew in body and spirit and lived in the deserts until the time of his public appearing. The witnesses agree the hidden years of wilderness preparation fitted him, free from the world’s allurements and strong in the Spirit, for his bold ministry of repentance. They see in his obscure growth the providence that shaped the forerunner in secret, the child kept and strengthened by God until the appointed day when he should come forth to prepare the way of the Lord before Israel. Clarke notes the law did not permit a man to enter the public ministry before thirty years (compare Numbers 4:3); the witnesses agree the hidden years of wilderness preparation fitted John, free from the world’s allurements and strong in the Spirit, for his bold ministry of repentance, the providence of God shaping the forerunner in secret until the appointed day of his showing unto Israel. Bede notes the future preacher of repentance passed his first years in the deserts that he might the more boldly reclaim his hearers from the world; the witnesses agree the hidden years of wilderness preparation, strong in the Spirit, fitted John for his bold ministry, kept by God until the day of his showing unto Israel.
